The Colosseum doesn’t try to impress — it simply stands there, weathered and unapologetic, letting its presence do the work. From a distance, it feels iconic; up close, it feels heavy with stories. The stone, worn smooth by time, carries a quiet gravity that photos can’t capture.

Walking inside shifts the experience. You’re no longer looking at a monument, but standing within a structure built for spectacle, noise, and emotion. The scale becomes clearer, not just in height but in intention. It’s easy to imagine the roar of the crowd, the tension, the drama — and equally easy to feel uneasy about it. That contrast is what gives the Colosseum its power.

What’s striking is how open it feels. Sunlight pours through broken arches, casting irregular shadows that change as you move. Nothing feels sealed off or polished. History is exposed, incomplete, and honest. The underground areas, where visible, add another layer — a reminder of the machinery and planning behind the games.

Despite the crowds outside, there’s a stillness in certain corners, moments where you can pause and absorb the weight of what happened here. The Colosseum isn’t just a Roman landmark; it’s a reflection of ambition, entertainment, cruelty, and endurance. It leaves you impressed, unsettled, and thoughtful — often all at once.
Grand, impressive and a quiet reminder that public entertainment used to involve considerably more violence. Dark, fascinating, and impossible to skip when you’re in Rome. Be there early.

The Colosseum in Rome is an absolute must-visit, a jaw-dropping relic of ancient engineering that transports you straight back to gladiator times. We arrived around 12:30 PM with skip-the-line tickets including arena floor and hypogeum access, and even with the midday crowds it was worth every penny—standing on the arena floor where lions and fighters once clashed gave me chills, while the underground tunnels revealed the dramatic stagecraft with trapdoors and hidden paths that made spectacles so thrilling. The Gate of Death and Gladiator Gate added a grim historical punch, evoking the brutal reality of those games, and climbing to the upper tiers offered panoramic views of the vast interior stretching toward the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, perfect for soaking in the scale despite the bustle. The iconic outer facade with its three stories of arches in Doric, Ionic, and Corinthian styles still stands majestic amid some restoration scaffolding, a true testament to Roman ingenuity under Emperors Vespasian and Titus. Pro tip: pair it with the Forum and Palatine on the same ticket, grab an audio guide to bring the stories alive without a group tour, and always book timed entry ahead via the official site since peaks hit hard around noon. The atmosphere and sheer immensity are unmatched—5 stars for any history buff!

First before you go look at U tube …. not Rick Steves who is pedantic and monotonous. Find the history of the COLOSSEUM or a small documentary before you go because it will be worth it. You know this is where Nero’s golden palace was. Vespasian made sure it was replaced by this engineering marvel of entertainment. Guides aren’t that good or they don’t have the depth. A Utube video will give you what you need to understand the historical and social significance of this MONUMENT of death. Yes, DEATH. THERE WERE ANIMAL HUNTS, persecuted souls and then the top act of the day were the GLADIATORS. A great movie to give you the dynamic and physical effects of the day can be seen in the movie GLADIATOR.

ALSO, there were Naval battles fought or reenacted by the flooding of the Arena. By the way arena comes from the Latin word harena… the h is silent….for SAND. Sand was a great substance to absorb blood.

Seating was done by class and sex. There also was an awning or valarium to partly cover the stadium which were handled by Roman sailors who had the training of manipulating the sails.

Moreover the name COLOSSEO came from the giant colossus of a statue of Nero. Remember his home the Golden Palace was there.

Another historical fact is this area was probably cleared by the famous fire in 64 AD maybe set by Nero or those he ordered to do so.

Travertine marble was used on the outside to give it its imperial look. Vespasian started it and his son finished it. Amazingly built in 8 years. The games were free and a gift of entertainment from the Emperor. The games were known as BREAD AND CIRCUS because citizens were also given bread. We all remember the Hollywood movies that made this place famous by the persecution of Christians. I think it’s worth buying an entrance ticket to see and feel the enormity and gravity of this place.

A fact to correct from the movies was the thumbs up/ down condemnation or released.
Thumbs up meant DEATH. Repeat DEATH. THINK OF THIS VISUAL: a thumbs up represented a sword being thrusted up into the victims body.

Absolutely Breathtaking! A Must-Do Experience in Rome (with Tips!)

My visit to the Colosseum in May 2025 was, without a doubt, one of the best experiences of my life. It's an absolutely breathtaking monument and a true highlight of any trip to Rome. The sheer scale and incredible history that permeate every stone are simply awe-inspiring. Standing in the arena where gladiators once fought is a feeling I’ll never forget.
A few months later, I'm taking the time to write this review and share some key tips for anyone hoping to have a similarly incredible experience. My friend and I were incredibly lucky to get two tickets for the guided Full Experience, including the underground section, in Italian! These tickets are notoriously difficult to get, but here's what I learned.

Tips for Getting Tickets
• Book on the Official Website: This is the most important tip. Other tour operators might charge you 2-3 times the price for the exact same tickets. Go straight to the official site if you can.
• Be Ready at 8:00 AM Rome Time: Tickets are released exactly at 8:00 AM (Rome time). Be logged on and ready to go!
• Be EXTREMELY Quick: Once you see the tickets you want, you have to be lightning fast to add them to your cart. People have a very limited time to complete the transaction, so this might be your only shot.
• Be Flexible: We couldn't get tickets for the Tuesday we wanted, but we were able to get two on Wednesday. Flexibility is key.
• Consider a Weekday Visit: Visiting on a weekday might slightly increase your chances of finding available tickets.
• Don't Refresh the Page: Resist the urge to refresh if you don't see what you want. The website will block you as a security measure if you refresh too many times.
• Try an Italian Language Tour: If you speak some Italian (or are willing to use a translation app), the Italian language tours are often less popular and might be easier to book than the English ones.
The Full Experience ticket is a great value. It gives you access to the undergrounds, the arena floor, and the regular second floor. What's even better is that it also includes access to the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, and this part of the ticket is valid for 48 hours.
Our tour in Italian was fantastic, and our guide was incredibly knowledgeable and warm. She shared fascinating details about the history of the Colosseum and the daily lives of the gladiators. I was happy to translate for my friend so he didn't miss out on any of the amazing information.
Overall, it was a spectacular and truly unforgettable experience. The Colosseum is the most beautiful and popular historical site for a reason, but a little planning is needed before the visit. Don't miss the opportunity to visit this incredible piece of history!

This is well worth the wait but book online! Be careful as there are lots of operators that promise guided tours and some do but others just issue you a ticket and the tell you to download the app! These tickets prices are well inflated so buy in advance from the official website. Wear comfortable shoes. ID is required to enter.

No matter how many YouTube videos you have watched, it's a totally different experience being inside the Colosseum in person.

We visited the Colosseum in Rome recently, and it truly is an amazing historic site — a must-see when in the city. The structure is breathtaking and full of fascinating history. However, it’s very busy, with large crowds almost all the time, so be prepared for that.

We booked our tickets through the official website, which was straightforward and cost €18 each. Make sure you bring ID with you, as it’s checked at the entrance.

If you want to visit the dungeons/underground area, those tickets need to be booked well in advance — we didn’t manage to get them this time, so it’s worth planning ahead if that’s on your list.

A useful tip for visitors: the toilets are located right after you enter, and once you move further inside, you can’t go back to them. The next available toilets are only after you exit the Colosseum, so it’s worth keeping that in mind before starting your tour.

Overall, it was a fantastic experience and one we’d definitely recommend — just make sure to plan ahead, book early, and allow plenty of time to explore this incredible piece of history! 🏛️🇮🇹

Would recommend going early summer or fall to escape the crowds and the heat and go early when it opens to avoid all the crowds and toward inside the colosseum as well. Be careful online where u buy your tickets from there will be a lot of scams out there!

Booked a tour. Did the underground portion which was AMAZING! Well worth the extra money to see under the stage and stage level.  If you go during the summer, bring a portable fan and umbrella to shade you from the sun.  A must see in Rome.

Visiting the Colosseum of El Djem was an incredible experience. Unlike its famous counterpart in Rome, it’s refreshingly uncrowded, giving you the freedom to explore at your own pace. You can truly soak in the atmosphere and imagine the gladiatorial past without being rushed or surrounded by crowds.

The site is beautifully restored and well maintained. You can walk through the structure, climb the tiers, and really immerse yourself in the history. Parking is easy—right in front of the entrance—and the whole visit feels relaxed and accessible.

If you want the full Roman amphitheater experience without the chaos of Rome, El Djem is a must. It’s authentic, peaceful, and unforgettable.

I would recommend buying tickets in advance. The line to get in moved fast and was only around 10 minutes. The organization of the staff and directions where to go is incredible. Even with a basic ticket you get to see a lot of parts of the place.

Visiting the Colosseum was an absolutely unforgettable experience! From the moment you see it, the grandeur and history leave you speechless. Standing inside such an iconic monument of the Roman Empire truly feels like traveling back in time.

The organization was excellent, everything was well managed, and the historical information provided was fascinating and enriching. Every corner of the Colosseum tells a powerful story and gives you a deep appreciation for Roman history and architecture.

If you are visiting Rome, this is an absolute must-see. A breathtaking experience that will stay with you forever!

Book well in advance. I didn't and paid for a guided tour... However the guided tour got us queue jumping everywhere and into some areas not covered in general access. Guides are knowledgeable, interesting, and funny. Well worth it when in Roma.

When visiting Rome for the first time you need to see the coliseum. It is a must do. And the best way to do it in my opinion is with a tour guide. Having this allowed us to get through all the queues really quickly despite how many people were there. The tour guide we had knew so much about the place and its history- even did his own gladiator fighting scene. The building is stunning and so well preserved, being able to see the inside was a privilege and worth doing. So in short, very very busy because everyone would want to go here, so you need to be organised and prepared.

Visiting the Colosseum was one of the most unforgettable experiences of my trip to Rome. Standing in front of this massive ancient structure truly takes your breath away it’s hard to believe it was built nearly 2,000 years ago and still stands so majestically today. The architecture, the atmosphere, and the history that surrounds every stone make it a must-see for anyone visiting Italy.

Walking through the arches and corridors, you can almost imagine the roar of the crowds and the gladiators preparing for battle. The underground areas, where animals and fighters once waited, are fascinating and worth exploring if you get a guided tour. The view from the upper levels offers a stunning panorama of ancient Rome and the Roman Forum especially beautiful in the early morning light or near sunset.

Everything is well organized, but it can get very crowded, so booking tickets online in advance is highly recommended. The audio guide or guided tours make a huge difference, bringing the history to life with stories and facts you wouldn’t learn otherwise.

Overall, the Colosseum is more than just a monument it’s a living reminder of human achievement, resilience, and the passage of time. A true wonder of the world and an absolute must-visit when in Rome.

It was impressive to see parts of ancient buildings dating back approximately 2,700 years. I had no idea the entire archaeological complex was so vast, nor that it held so many attractions for those who love and admire the history of what generations long before ours did. It's a place you shouldn't miss if you come to Rome.
Amazing place. A lot of tourists, but the view is absolutely awesome. Look out for small street vendors who try to sell you everything — stay alert and you’ll be fine.

An unforgettable experience! The Colosseum is the only world wonder I’ve visited that was completely free just make sure to check which days offer free entry. Be prepared though: the line can get very long, especially on those free days, and the Roman sun is no joke bring water and wear a hat. Despite the wait, standing inside this ancient structure is truly awe inspiring and 100% worth it. Would definitely recommend timing your visit early in the day!

Beautiful place and history, we were glade to participate in the tour, out tour guide was great. Wonderful place, very busy. We were able to get loads of pictures as our tour guide was very happy to take pictures for us. I highly recommend doing the tour mainly with a guid3 as you get lots of info.

Of course the view was so pretttyyy. The first pic is this year the day before the new metro was to open and the second is from some years ago. If you wanna go in for free you should go first Sunday of the month. But you should prepare for the big (very long) line, but fortunately the speed is also fast so maybe like a 20-30 min of waiting. Enjoy!!

An absolute must-see! Be sure to book your tickets in advance and go for the skip-the-line option—I never waited more than 10 minutes, and it’s totally worth it for such an iconic experience.

I highly recommend getting the audio guide. It allows you to explore at your own rhythm, which I much prefer over a guided tour. While having a guide might offer more context, I love the freedom to take my time and absorb the atmosphere.

The Colosseum’s history is nothing short of awe-inspiring. It’s hard to believe such a structure was built so long ago. While it stands as a glorious monument today, you can’t help but think about the brutal events that once took place within its walls. Close your eyes and imagine it packed to capacity—wow. The energy, the chaos, the spectacle… it’s chilling and fascinating all at once.

What else can I say? It’s a mystical and unforgettable place that truly brings history to life.

Must see in Rome.
It is worth buying arena and second floor tickets in advance ( be there 10-15 minutes before your entrance time, the line is not long at all, the security check is about 10-15 minutes) as this allows you to see almost the entire building. The underground tour is not included, but we didn't miss it. It is definitely worth seeing at sunset or after dark, as it is beautifully lit.
Unmissable attraction. You shouldn't come to Rome and not visit the Colosseum.

We lucked out, attending on a Sunday when admission was free. We did a self-guided tour. What an incredible ruin, with access to so many views of the interior and exterior. Seeing how the building was constructed and how it has been altered and partially restored through the years was very educational.

The entire grounds are incredible. (Watch out for pickpockets!)

An unforgettable place in Rome. I was amazed by the architecture, the scale, and the incredible history behind it. Standing inside felt like stepping back in time. A truly iconic and breathtaking monument.

In ancient times, gladiator fights and public games were held in the Colosseum.
It is considered the largest ancient amphitheater in the world.
In 2007, it was included among the New Seven Wonders of the World.
Today, it is a symbol of Italy and one of the country’s biggest tourist attractions.
Glorious experience. My expectations were high and they surpassed it. The amount of history that was left behind was amazing.

Before anything, make sure you prebook the tickets to enter the colosseum, as without this the queuing will be extremely strenous.

The Colosseum doesn’t need much introduction, it is spectacular by nature. That being said there are a few things to note. The first is that if you are going with the ‘full access with arena’ do note that you do NOT get access to the underground, that is a separate higher tier package.

There also multiple other options it seems which allow you to go beyond the second floor of the colosseum as we saw some few people get to the top floors.

Second point to note is that you will most likely be told to do the Palantine Hill and Roman Forum first if booking the site vis tourist sites. We booked for 2.30 planning to do the Colosseum first while the sun is high, then towards the Forum and Hill once the sun goes down.

We were directed to do the other two first at the peak of sun, with the Colosseum booking actually happening at 4.15. So be careful with the booking to avoid the scorching sun if possible.

You can also great great solo empty pics in the morning here before the crowds if you arrive before 7am as we did. The rest of the day will not have anytime when the area is as empty again.

Lastly, wear extremely comfortable clothing and shoes, as doing all three will be well over 10.000 steps and a total of 2-3 hours of continuous walking.
Wow, so cool to stand inside the colosseum! Make reservations at least 2 to 3 months in advance and get access to the lower levels too!

Absolutely awe-inspiring! Walking through the Colosseum feels like stepping back in time. The history, architecture, and sheer size are breathtaking. We purchase tickets for a introductory tour which gave us the history, it was very informational. A must-see when visiting Rome — truly unforgettable!

First Visit to the Colosseum – A Must-See in Rome!
We finally made it to the Colosseum, and it did not disappoint! It’s absolutely a must when visiting Rome. Since it’s summertime, the lines are long—even with advance tickets. And the tour groups? They’re everywhere and a bit overwhelming.

We opted for an evening visit, which helped a little with the crowds, but it was still packed. About a month before our trip, we tried to score underground tour tickets—waking up at 3 AM U.S. time and checking every 15 minutes—but they sold out instantly every time. That part was a bit of a disappointment.

Still, being there in person was incredible. It’s one of those places that makes you feel the weight of history just by standing in it.

It is a truly magnificent place with so much history in it. To stand there and see all the architecture that it has taken place centuries ago is a great feeling. There's a lot of information and items that they have found in the Colosseum and are displayed just before you enter the amphitheater in the centre. All the details are in English too which that is very helpful to get to know the history of it.   Do purchase tickets beforehand.
Amazing place to go and so much bigger than expected. The security on getting in is farcical, but just go with it. Definitely a must in Rome

We purchased tour tickets in advance and had a very knowledgeable guide. It is a crowded tourist spot, and having a guide was helpful.

We visited on a very hot day. There is not a lot of shade. Bring along a fan, umbrella, or water bottle.

Seeing everything up close is amazing! Original travertine and cobblestone are in place and uneven in many spots. There is also a lot of walking involved. Dress comfortably and accordingly.

I don’t need to tell anything! You already know that this is the most popular and impressive attraction of the Capital of Italy, Rome and the entire country! If you want to visit it inside you need to buy tickets (online). Also the view is magical!!! I recommend it without any doubt! I don’t think I need to tell you more, you know how incredible it is!

An absolute highlight in Rome. My strongest recommendation: don’t just book a ticket — book a guided tour (ideally combined with the Forum Romanum and Palatine Hill). It makes a huge difference. Do yourself a favor, DO NOT consider to wait in line to buy a ticket on the spot :)

With the guide, we got historical background already on the way, and inside he explained not only the facts but also clarified some myths we all know from movies. Even if you’re familiar with Roman history, you’ll definitely have a few “aha moments.”

The other big advantage: skipping the lines. The regular waiting lines were incredibly long, even for those with pre-booked tickets. With the guide, we still went through security checks, but all in all it took only around 15 minutes to get in.

Inside, the Colosseum is breathtaking. I highly recommend including access to the arena floor — from there you can also see into the lower parts, which really adds to the experience. And don’t miss the Forum Romanum and Palatine Hill right next door; together it makes the visit complete and unforgettable.

Practical tip: in summer, bring sunscreen, a hat or cap, or even a small umbrella. A big part of the tour is outdoors, and the sun can be intense.
Amazing!  Also known as Flavian Amphitheater. Actually a pretty morbid place when you research the history. But its HISTORY ❤️

Incredible! Buy your tickets in advance. We had tickets right at opening and for the first 15 minutes it felt like we were the only ones inside.

Also they dont respect the time entry so show up like 40 minutes before to line up it gets very long very quick.
Colosseum is the one of the historical places where you have to be at least once in the lifetime. Unbelievable feeling and you would need a professional guide to understand the history even more.

Still in time. Different after 20 years only by the people, the security, ticket prices, the need to pre-order, the gates and sadly no gatti di Roma. But it will always be as impressive, even after we are long gone...

Booked a guided tour through GetYourGuide which included the Colosseum, Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, and it was excellent from start to finish. The Colosseum itself is incredible to see in person, and the guide was fantastic—really knowledgeable and engaging. Met some great people in the group too, which made the experience even better. Well worth doing!

"I had a fantastic experience here and would highly recommend it to anyone looking for quality and great service. From the moment I arrived, the atmosphere was welcoming and the staff was incredibly professional, attending to every detail with care. It is rare to find a place that consistently hits the mark on both efficiency and friendliness, but this spot definitely nailed it. Everything was well-organized, clean, and exactly as described. I am beyond satisfied with my visit and will certainly be returning in the future. A solid five-star experience all around!"
Pay for a guide head of time, wear comfortable shoes, there’s steep stairs but also an elevator. Wait times are shorter in a guided group.

You must buy a ticket a month in advance. Don't be surprised seeing a looooong line. It goes really quick (5 mins maybe).fantastic experience ! I wish to buy "full experience " ticket including underground and the top level but tickets are unavailable.

We took an organized tour, including access to the area made to reflect the arena. Access was smooth, but with many checkpoints. Guide (archaeologist Valerio) provided interesting facts that allowed us to appreciate the former scale better. Coupled to a tour of Palatine and Forum Romanum. Not to be missed.

An interesting place where you can travel back in time to the days of ancient Rome.
You can really imagine what once took place here. Your ticket also includes entry to the Roman Forum. You don’t need to spend too much time inside the Colosseum — plan more time for the Forum. Ticket bought via Booking.com

The entry process for the Colosseum is quite complicated, with several gates and baggage scanners. But it’s worth visiting at least once. I highly recommend taking an evening walk around the Colosseum — the structure is beautifully illuminated.
Beautiful and breathtaking, definitely recommend getting tickets to see. Cost around €18/23 for cheap tickets

This is my second visit to Colosseum over the years and I am always stoked when I am here.

A must on the list to visit when your are in Rome.

Arena built by emperors Vespasian & Titus (70-80 AD), famous for hosting gladiator fights, animal hunts, & public spectacles for up to 80,000 spectators.

The Colosseum is one of the most awe-inspiring landmarks in the world. Standing in front of it, you can immediately feel the weight of history and imagine the thousands of stories that unfolded within its walls. The scale is breathtaking – from the massive outer structure to the intricate details inside.

Walking through the arena and underground passages gives you a sense of what life was like in ancient Rome. It’s not just a monument, but a living reminder of the Roman Empire’s greatness and architectural genius.

I highly recommend booking tickets in advance and joining a guided tour to really understand the history and hidden details. The experience is unforgettable, especially when you stand in the middle and look up at the tiers of seats that once held tens of thousands of spectators.

A visit to Rome wouldn’t be complete without the Colosseum. It’s powerful, historic, and simply unforgettable.
Must visit place. We went with a guided tour. The tour guide used to be a school teacher. He gave us so much backgrounds and information and definitely worth it.

What an architectural masterpiece! Just thinking about how and when it was built, with what kind of planning and execution, with the technology available back then makes it even more fascinating than it already is. Imagine, 80.000 people gathered in it, massive even for today's standards. But then again, we cheer it for beeing there and reminding us of the great Roman Empire, but one should not forget also the events that occured in it, and perhaps pay an hommage to those who suffered there.
A must see when visiting Rome, the Colosseum look absolutely majestic from afar. Opposite to the Colosseum is a small hill with a small bridge where it's perfect for a pictue

"I am Maximus Decimus Meridius, Commander of the Armies of the North, General of the Felix Legions, loyal servant to the true emperor, Marcus Aurelius. Father to a murdered son, husband to a murdered wife. And I will have my vengeance, in this life or the next"

Just wonderful visit. No monument will ever top this one off. My pilgrimage is finally done and I could not be happier.

Loved this.  We did a skip the line guided tour of the ground floor and arena which included a tour of the Roman Forum.  It was amazing to be where the gladiators walked.  Book tickets early as only 500 people out of 30,000 people get to do this tour a day.

Nice Sunset time ❤️
There a Great Restaurant near the Colosseum called Il Giardino ai Fori ❤️
Lovely food ...lovely vibes.. lovely Staff and Music
We enjoyed Rome ❤️

One of those places you should visit at least once in your life. Despite the crowds, standing before this ancient ruin and appreciating the architecture of its time is a truly cultural and educational experience.

I mean, it's history. Take the actual tour so you can better understand what you're seeing. Buy tickets ahead of time. I think tickets include the forum, which you can go to within a day or so. We went the first week of May and the weather was great. It was one of the most crowded places we went to but it was still early in the season so it was manageable

Listen up, fellow time-hoppers: I portaled straight from 2025 into this bad boy, the Colosseum (or "Flavian Amphitheater" if you're feeling pedantic). Capacity? 50,000 plus screaming fans – bigger than Coachella, but with way more severed limbs and zero porta-potties.

Pros: Epic vibes!
Cons: None to speak of!

Would I go again? In a heartbeat... or whatever's left of it. 10/10 for chaos. This is where Rome's real drama lives.

I don't think I need say anything about this iconic structure! Save to say that there are better preserved examples elsewhere. We didn't go inside this time, so can't commentbon tickets, waiting times etc.

siting the Colosseum was like stepping back into ancient Roman history. The sheer size, architecture, and energy of the place gave me goosebumps. It’s incredible to imagine gladiator battles and public spectacles that once happened here nearly 2,000 years ago.

The guided tour gave deep insight into its history, and the underground levels were fascinating. Standing in the middle of such a powerful structure, I could feel the legacy of the Roman Empire all around me.

A must-visit landmark for anyone coming to Rome — powerful, majestic, and unforgettable.

Amazing place, all the pictures I have seen, do not pay justice to how Amazing it is in real life.
We visited on 1st Sunday of March, so tip for everyone...check before you visit as it might be free...especially on Sundays, this is why I do not recommend buying tickets online in advance, if you are visiting on a Sunday when it might be free. Beware that even though it is free, you should join the queu around 7am as it will get long quite quickly and early.

Beautiful historic architectural piece of art. Always a pleasure to visit and experience the magnificence of history. How many stories hide beneath the walls of the colosseum. Excelent and brutal place and the same time.

Visiting the Colosseum was an unforgettable experience! We booked a guided tour with Enjoy Rome, and it was totally worth it. Having priority tickets made everything so much easier - we skipped the long lines and got access to privileged viewing spots inside. I definitely recommend buying your tickets in advance; otherwise, you’ll end up waiting in a huge queue. A must-do in Rome!

The Colosseum is absolutely stunning and one of the highlights of visiting Rome. The scale of the structure is breathtaking, and standing inside gives you a real sense of the history and grandeur of ancient times. It’s incredible to imagine the events that took place here centuries ago.

The site is well-organized for visitors, with helpful signs and guided tours that make it easy to learn about the history and significance of the amphitheater. The views from different levels are spectacular, especially when you look down into the arena or out toward the Roman Forum. Definitely worth booking tickets in advance to avoid long lines. A must-see landmark that truly brings the past to life.

Breathtaking. Incredible antique architectural
Jewel in the center of Rome. During the day, can be very busy with lots and lots of tourist so you may need to wait to get good pictures. On the other side; a bit later you will see the incredible lights on and maybe less crowded for better pictures.
I’m still amazed La
Buy your ticket in advance and be there on the exact time given to avoid waiting. Magnificent place!
It was such a fun experience exploring the Colosseum and our guide was very informative/well versed in everything we asked her about.
You have to see this when in Rome. Beautiful place to see and walk through the stadium. It actually feels surreal to go in. I recommend going in with a tour guide as it can be very overwhelming.
Ι got there using Roma Pass. Though it was very crowdy it only took ten minutes to enter. Impressive views of the famous monument.

Visiting the Colosseum in Rome was an amazing experience. I highly recommend buying your ticket in advance and choosing a guided tour. Having a guide makes a huge difference—you learn so many interesting facts. The guide also points out small details you might otherwise miss, both inside the Colosseum and in the surrounding area. It truly adds depth and meaning to the visit
So cool day or night! Get tickets to go!! It felt so magical to be there. Listen to the gladiator soundtrack while here for an experience!

Wow! What an amazing site to visit and see in person, highly recommend buying tickets ahead of time to go. Grab a guided tour from Crown Tours up the street at a reasonable cost and you get to see every bit of it, while understanding all the history and stories. Very cool experience! We went mid day at 1:30 and it was busy and very hot, I wouldn’t recommend going then.
Well worth a visit. We had an excellent guide. A very hot day i would recommend going as early as possible.

Visiting the Colosseum is an unforgettable experience – its monumental scale is breathtaking and makes you wonder how such a marvel was built almost two thousand years ago. It truly sparks the imagination and captures the spirit of ancient Rome. My only regret is that I couldn’t access the top floor, which would have made the visit even more incredible.

It's a pleasure just walking around this historic place! Something to see everywhere. Imagine life 2000 years ago. Learn about it. A hooded crow was my friend. Today: be aware of friendly strangers on the street who may trick you into accepting a gift. Ignore them. Do not respond nor acknowledge. Look away. Keep walking. If they harras you, tell him you'll call the carrabinieri (police).

It is so worth it seeing it in person, I won't lie to you. The admission is not expensive a regular pass is under €20 and gives you access to everything except the arena. The lines move fairly quickly and the exit is a maze but fantastic experience overall. If you have time dedicate an hour or your Rome trip to exploring the colosseum in person.

One of the seven wonders of the world and a huge and a marvelous place. Recommended to get fast track skip the line tickets as the line for normal access can get too Long especially if you are with kids. Washroom inside is free to use.
Tip: Real picturesque photos will come when you are exiting the Colosseum.
You can get a panoramic view of the Colosseum from the top of the metro station opposite to it.
You really can’t miss the colosseum. We did the self guided tour inside and it was great. Busy but plenty of space for everyone. A must do for anyone visiting Rome.

A massive historical sites to explore the Roma's early days of history.  Sometimes gets really busy and uneven surfaces to walk around, therefore recommended to book the tickets or tour in advance and wear a comfortable but safe shoes 👞 for the uneven surfaces around the place.  Some water fountains are around but limited WC / public toilets facilities.  Also be careful about the big crowds for safety and potential hazards.

Very cool. Personally, don’t go through this place with a tour guide. Do an audio guide and explore this place on your own, they will explain a lot of things you won’t want and take longer to walk through this place than you need.

The Colosseum is pure magic, no matter how many times I visit, it never loses its power to move me. Standing before it, I feel connected to centuries of history, to the people who once filled its stands, and to the timeless beauty of Rome itself. I’ve been there countless times during my stay in the city, sometimes just to sit nearby and watch the sunset reflect off its ancient stones. Every visit feels like the first, filled with awe and emotion. It’s more than a monument, it’s a feeling, a heartbeat of Rome that stays with you forever. If you’re in Italy, you should definitely stop in Rome, and visit this iconic place.

Highly recommend book a guided tour well in advance of your trip. We were able to skip the line and go right in! It was amazing to hear about the history of this famous place and see it with my own eyes!

An absolute must if you are in Rome, explore the different floors with the upper floor also offering great views of the surrounding area. We didn't book tickets in advance so were a bit worried but managed to get tickets with only about 10-15 minutes of queuing and then straight in for 18 euros. Need to be careful on the way in as people with tourist guide batches will offer to get you in quicker and with a guided tour but charge way over the odds so would suggest play it safe and book online in advance.
Very busy, but very neat to be where millions have been over the centuries.

A brutal reminder of ancient Roman power and spectacle. Walking through its corridors makes history feel immediate and raw. Combine it with the Roman Forum for full context. Expect heavy crowds and aggressive vendors outside. Go early morning to avoid chaos and heat.

Beautifully maintained piece of history and quite reasonably priced entry fee. Security line moves smoothly and without much of hiccup.

Lots of different parts to explore and there's no time limit on how long you can spend there.

Definitely make sure to take a hat and also a poncho as you are not protected from any elements!

The Colosseum is a must-see in Rome—huge, historic, and impressive. Definitely buy your ticket well in advance to avoid sellouts. Give yourself about 1.5 hours to explore, and consider an audio guide if you’re into history. Wear comfy shoes; there’s a fair bit of walking and climbing. Well worth the visit!

I have been to the Arena in Arles. The scale and history of Colosseo is impeccable.
The experience would be better if the tours weren’t blocking the route or speaking so loudly like giving a lecture.

When leaving the Colosseo, the sellers outside the Colosseo said ‘I like your shoes’ to attract and stop me, they even griped my shoulder which was shocking to me. Perhaps the management can take care of that…

Love the atmosphere. Feels like 2000 years ago. Colosseum is big enough so even with huge crowds people fit inside with no problem. As an EU citizen between the age 18-25, I had to pay just 2 euros for the ticket.
Was able to attend the underground tour. Our tour guide was very informative. Worth the price.

Romes greatest and most iconic place to visit. You can see it best from the museum in the middle of the city if you go up to the terrace level (see picture) but we did go inside. You must have a passport with you, there are x-ray machines and metal detectors. If you can go with the least amount of stuff, you will have no problem. It's really something. The self tour is 75 minutes long inside and it exits to Constantine's arc. Very neat and lots to see.

A must see in Rome. It was Magnificent. They have different types of tickets that allowed you access to different areas dependingon the price. Arrive at the time your entrance is scheduled for. They are very strict and won't allowed you to go in earlier than your scheduled time. You MUST buy your tickets in advance. If someone is selling you tickes for the same day it is likely a scam. There are guided tours. We did our own, followed the crowd and it was a great experience. Avoid going during the summer months, we went in July and it was extremely hot. There is No parking nearby or public washroom.

Visiting the Colosseum was an absolute highlight of my trip to Rome, and it truly lived up to its reputation as a must-see. The history behind this ancient marvel is incredible, making it, in my opinion, the best ancient sight in the city. The sheer scale and stories of gladiators and events from centuries past are awe-inspiring.

The queue was manageable, and the crowd control was impressive, which made the experience smooth and enjoyable. However, a word of caution: I tried booking tickets through a third-party site, Headout, which canceled my tickets just days before, costing me access to the arena floor—such a disappointment! Tickets on the official Colosseum website sell out fast, so book early and stick to the official source if possible.

Despite the ticketing hiccup, the Colosseum is a breathtaking experience, and I’d highly recommend it to anyone visiting Rome. Just plan ahead to avoid any booking stress!
This was amazing, done so well. Lots of walking but worth every step! A must visit.

Great experience.

I am not sure what the status of the arena floor's reconstruction is but you can see an edge of it was reconstructed when you are on the inside.

Also, you are looking at the exposed underground in the absence of the arena floor.

One note:
The steps to get to the second floor are quite steep. There's an elevator for easier accessibility for those who need assistance.
Unfortunately, I couldn't see it from the inside because the tickets were sold out. I recommend buying them early online.
We took the bullet train from Florence to Rome to see the Coliseum! I highly recommend this! So much beautiful countryside to see! The Coliseum is mind blowing! Wear comfy shoes! Alot of walking!
Amazing ruin that shows how far - and not so far - we have developed as the human race. Very inspiring.
The stadium is ginormous so If you are in a rush, you can take pictures even a quick selfie, just from the outside without entering the ticketed area.

Amazing place to see history.  Not good for strollers, wheelchairs or scooters.  Roads are rocky and bumpy, but adds to the atmosphere.  Pictures do no justice to the absolute beauty of this structure.  Definately a buckling place
Nice there but busy lots of people would recommend booking your tickets online first

From the pages of my school books to standing right in front of it. Seeing this UNESCO World Heritage site in real life is a dream come true. Truly a surreal moment that will stay with me forever.
🤎🤎🤎

My girlfriend and I moved to Rome as students, and even though we had seen it before, we couldn’t resist visiting the Colosseum just two days after arriving. It’s such a unique and surreal structure — even the exterior alone is incredibly captivating, and the inside is on a whole different level. No recommendation needed — you already know. It’s something everyone should see at least once in their lifetime.
It was absolutely amazing! Words don't describe the colostrum. I tour guide was amazing

The Colosseum in Rome is one of the most iconic landmarks I’ve ever visited.
Its massive scale and historical importance are simply breathtaking.
Walking through the ancient arena makes you imagine the grandeur of Roman spectacles.
The structure is incredibly well-preserved and impressive from every angle.
Photos don’t do justice to the feeling of standing inside it.
Arriving early helps avoid crowds and enjoy the views peacefully.
This is a must-see for anyone visiting Rome.
Truly an unforgettable experience full of wonder and history.
Just amazing. A must.  Definitely have a guide, it's been standing for over 2000 years! Just so much to take in. Book in advance and take the skip the queues.
There are multiple entrances but only one you can use depending on your ticket.  They were helpful in telling you which one to go to.  Awesome to go inside the arena.

The Colosseum is one of the wonders of the world. To see it, to touch it, to walk it's halls, to stand where so much history was made is such a moving experience.

You can almost hear the roar of 50,000 spectators, the clash of gladiators, and the drama that once unfolded on the arena floor. ⚔️🦁

The architectural details are fascinating — from the underground chambers (hypogeum) where animals and fighters awaited, to the tiered seating that reflected Roman social hierarchy. I visited in the early morning to avoid crowds and the midday heat. The golden sunlight filtering through the arches was magical. 🌞 Don’t miss the upper levels — the panoramic views of Rome and the Roman Forum are stunning. 🏛️🌇

The Colosseum isn’t just a ruin; it’s a living reminder of Rome’s rich past and a UNESCO World Heritage Site that still inspires awe today.

A must-visit for every history lover and traveler.⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Visiting the Colosseum in Rome is like stepping straight into the pages of history. This monumental amphitheater, almost two thousand years old, still dominates the skyline with its sheer scale and architectural brilliance. Standing before it, you immediately sense why it has become one of the most iconic landmarks in the world.

The experience begins even before you enter: walking around the exterior lets you appreciate the intricate arches and weathered stones, each bearing witness to centuries of events, from gladiatorial games to medieval reuse. Inside, the atmosphere is equally captivating. The exposed underground chambers reveal how complex the original structure was, with hidden passages and elevators once used to stage the games. It’s humbling to realize that tens of thousands of spectators once gathered here, cheering as history unfolded below.

Practical aspects are worth mentioning. Booking tickets online in advance is highly recommended, especially during peak tourist season, as lines can be long. Guided tours or audio guides greatly enrich the visit, offering fascinating details about the Colosseum’s construction, its social role in ancient Rome, and the stories of the people who fought or worked there. The upper levels provide stunning views, both of the arena floor and of the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill nearby.

The site is very accessible by public transport, with the Colosseo metro stop just steps away. Amenities include a well-organized security check, friendly staff, and clear signage in multiple languages. While it can get crowded, the sense of wonder far outweighs any inconvenience.

Overall, the Colosseum is an unforgettable experience—a must-see for anyone visiting Rome. It’s not just a ruin; it’s a living reminder of human ingenuity, history, and the enduring legacy of Roman culture. Highly recommended for history lovers, photographers, and anyone who wants to feel connected to the ancient world.

The Colosseum – A Journey Through Time
Rome, Italy

Visiting the Colosseum is like stepping into the bloodstream of history—where echoes of emperors, gladiators, and roaring crowds still seem to pulse through the ancient stone. It’s not just a monument; it’s a symbol of Rome’s eternal spirit, architectural brilliance, and turbulent past. No visit to the Eternal City is complete without standing in its shadow.

🕰️ Historical Depth That Resonates
Constructed between 72 and 80 AD under the Flavian emperors, the Colosseum—originally called the Flavian Amphitheatre—was the largest amphitheatre ever built at the time and remains the largest still standing today. Capable of holding up to 50,000 to 80,000 spectators, it was the heart of Roman public life.

Here, gladiators fought to the death. Wild animals from across the empire—lions, elephants, crocodiles—were brought in to entertain the crowds. Mock naval battles (naumachiae) were sometimes staged in the arena, flooding the floor with water. Public executions, epic dramas, and imperial propaganda all unfolded beneath its marble arches.

Despite centuries of earthquakes, looting, and pollution, the Colosseum endures as a powerful symbol of both imperial grandeur and the darker complexities of Roman entertainment.

🎯 The Visitor Experience
🌅 First Impressions
Whether you arrive from the Colosseo Metro station or wander over from the Roman Forum, your first glimpse of the Colosseum is unforgettable. Towering over the surrounding ruins, it seems almost mythological. Its pockmarked façade tells the story of time: war, weather, and survival.

Take a moment to walk the perimeter before entering. From every angle, the architecture reveals new layers of symmetry, decay, and splendor.

🏟️ Inside the Arena
Once inside, the feeling is both overwhelming and exhilarating. The sheer scale of the amphitheatre is breathtaking. Looking down onto the exposed hypogeum—the underground network where animals and gladiators were kept—you can vividly imagine the tension of ancient spectacles.

If you've booked the arena floor access, you’ll stand where gladiators once fought, looking up into what would have been a deafening crowd. It’s a visceral, humbling moment.

Climb to the upper levels and you’re rewarded with panoramic views of Rome and the adjacent Roman Forum, with Palatine Hill in the background. The stone seating, though weathered, reminds you of how sophisticated Roman engineering and crowd management truly were.

Visiting the Colosseum in Rome was an absolute dream come true for me, especially coming all the way from North Carolina. I’ve seen countless photos and documentaries over the years, but nothing compares to standing inside this incredible piece of history in person. The moment I walked in, I felt transported back nearly 2,000 years—imagining the crowds, the architecture, and the sheer scale of what once took place there.

The guided tour was outstanding and made the entire experience even more meaningful. Learning about the engineering behind the structure, the stories of the gladiators, and the cultural impact of the Colosseum gave me a new appreciation for Ancient Rome. The views from inside and outside are breathtaking—especially if you visit early in the morning before the crowds get too heavy.

If you’re visiting Rome, the Colosseum is an absolute must-see. It’s powerful, humbling, and inspiring all at once. As someone from North Carolina experiencing it for the first time, I can honestly say it exceeded every expectation. Don’t miss it!

The Colosseum is absolutely magnificent and a must-see in Rome. The exterior is breathtaking—walking around it gives you the best perspective of its size, history, and architecture. For me, the outside was actually more impressive than the inside. The interior is worth visiting, but it can feel a little overrated compared to the stunning view from outside.

That said, it’s still a once-in-a-lifetime experience, and standing inside such an ancient structure is unforgettable.

Tips for visitors:
• Buy your tickets online in advance to skip long lines.
• Go early in the morning or later in the afternoon to avoid the biggest crowds.
• Take time to walk all the way around the outside before entering—you’ll appreciate the scale much more.
• Wear comfortable shoes; there’s a lot of walking and uneven ground.

Visited the Colosseum in the summer of 2022. It was a great tour and definitely something you have to do if you’re in Rome. Because Italy still had stricter COVID measures at the time, the crowds were smaller than usual, which made it easier to walk through and actually take in the history without feeling rushed or packed in. Being inside and seeing the size of the arena and the underground passages was an incredible experience.

Outside the Colosseum you do have to watch out for common scams. People will come up offering roses, bottles of water, bracelets, or even try to get you to step on “art” laid out on the ground, then pressure you for money. Best advice is to just say no and keep walking.

As for food, a lot of the restaurants directly around the Colosseum are tourist traps. Prices are higher, the quality is hit-or-miss, and you’re paying more for the location than the meal. If you want a better dining experience, it’s worth walking 10-15 minutes away into the smaller side streets where you’ll find better food and more authentic spots at reasonable prices.

The Colosseum stands as one of the most iconic and awe-inspiring landmarks of the ancient world, a monumental reminder of the grandeur of the Roman Empire. Located in the heart of Rome, this विशाल amphitheatre continues to captivate millions of visitors with its scale, history, and architectural brilliance.

Built between 70–80 AD under emperors Vespasian and Titus, the Colosseum once hosted gladiatorial contests, animal hunts, and public spectacles that drew crowds of up to 50,000 spectators. Even in its partially ruined state, the structure’s engineering sophistication is remarkable. The intricate system of arches, corridors, and underground chambers—known as the hypogeum—offers a fascinating glimpse into ancient Roman ingenuity.

Walking through the Colosseum today is like stepping back in time. The worn stone steps, towering walls, and panoramic views from the upper tiers create a powerful sense of connection to history. Informational displays and guided tours help bring the stories of gladiators, emperors, and everyday Romans to life, making the visit both educational and immersive.

The surrounding area, including the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, adds further depth to the experience, allowing visitors to explore the broader context of ancient Roman civilization. Early morning or late afternoon visits are recommended to avoid crowds and enjoy better lighting for photography.

Despite heavy tourism, the Colosseum retains an undeniable aura of majesty and resilience. It is not just a monument, but a symbol of Rome’s enduring legacy. A visit here is essential for anyone interested in history, architecture, or simply experiencing one of the world’s greatest cultural treasures.

Visiting the Colosseum in Rome is an unforgettable experience and one of the highlights of any trip to the city. To make the most of your visit and avoid standing in long queues, it’s highly recommended to buy your tickets well in advance. Tickets and ID are checked carefully at the entrance, so make sure to have both ready. The best way to buy tickets is through the official website, which guarantees you a valid entry and often offers different types of passes. Also, if you are a European Union resident under 26 years old, you can get a much cheaper ticket — just remember to bring your ID to prove your age and nationality. The Colosseum itself is truly awe-inspiring. Walking through this ancient amphitheater, you can almost feel the history and grandeur of the Roman Empire come alive around you. The scale and architectural genius of the Colosseum are mind-blowing — it’s hard not to be amazed by how such a massive structure was built almost 2,000 years ago. Despite the large crowds, which sometimes make it a bit challenging to move around freely, the atmosphere is electric and totally worth it. Every corner tells a story, and the views from inside the arena are incredible. Overall, the Colosseum leaves a lasting impression and is a must-visit for anyone coming to Rome. Whether you’re fascinated by history, architecture, or simply want to experience one of the world’s most iconic landmarks, this ancient wonder will not disappoint. I highly recommend including it on your itinerary!

The Colosseum is the ultimate symbol of ancient Rome and a must-see for any visitor. Standing in its shadow, you can almost hear the roar of the crowds and imagine the epic gladiator battles that once took place here. Its sheer scale and architectural brilliance are awe-inspiring, with underground chambers and towering arches that tell stories of power, entertainment, and empire. A guided tour brings its history to life, from emperors to spectators. The views from the upper levels are incredible, and the whole experience is unforgettable. It’s a true marvel that captures the spirit of Rome’s grandeur and resilience.

The entry process was impressively organized. They checked everyone’s ticket times individually to let people in, ensuring no one jumped the queue—making everything very fair and smooth. Still, it’s a good idea to get in line about 15-20 minutes before your ticket time to avoid any hassle.

The ticket also included access to the Roman Forum, adding even more depth to the experience. Walking through these iconic ancient sites felt like stepping back in time — absolutely breathtaking and unforgettable. Definitely one of the highlights of our trip!

I have purchased my tickets on the official web pages exactly 30 days prior to planned visit , synchronized my time to AM Rome time. I initially wanted the attic, but as those were coming up as unavailable, I settled for 24hour Colosseum, Roman Forum, Palatine at 8:46. We arrived 15 mins early, and after going through security and 3 ticket checks, we were among the first visitors in, used the restrooms and then immediately walked up the staircase to 2nd floor. What an incredible feeling to experience an almost empty Colosseum. We walked all around soaking up the glorious views, visited the gift shop and then after 11/2 hours reluctantly left to join our tour group outside. I found the staff there helpful. Don't forget to fill up your water bottles right outside the entrance at the water kiosk,in addition to still water there was a cold delicious sparkling water for free.

It's a magnificent experience and a must-see spot in Rome. If it suits your schedule and budget, try arena ticket,  or underground/attic tickes, since crowds can kill the joy of first and second level of the standard ticket. Also, always buy the ticket online in advance, to avoid extremely long ticket queues.
one of my favorite places in Rome! I visit it every year and will continue to do so
Simply amazing and full of history. I would 100% recommend anyone to book a guided tour!

This ancient wonder still continues to draw people to its grand structure. You have timed entrances to the interior and the tickets cost 18 Euros. You have access to view the structure from the ground level and from the elevated floor. It is a must do for anyone visiting Rome. The exterior lends itself to be a stunning back drop for photos. One cannot but marvel at the level of effort and planning that would have gone to construct this monument. They also have a small museum which outlines in details on how it has transformed over the years.

The Colosseum in Rome is absolutely worth the visit, despite the challenges of heat and crowds during the summer. While the high season presents a few obstacles, the historical significance and impressive scale of the amphitheater make it a powerful and unforgettable experience.

The Colosseum is a breathtaking historical marvel, offering a glimpse into ancient Rome's grandeur.

The tickets queues can get very long very quickly so one can opt for skip-the-line tickets to avoid long queues.

You may consider exploring the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill nearby for a wholesome experience.

Guided tours are also available for in-depth storytelling and exclusive access to restricted areas like the underground chambers and arena floor. There are also night tours, which provide a unique perspective of the Colosseum under moonlight, making for a delightful, and quite different experience. All in all, the Colosseum is not to be missed.

No huge lines when I went, but skip-the-line or guided tickets help a lot. You learn more and save time.

Tip: Start with the Colosseum, then do Palatine Hill and finish at the Forum. Wear comfy shoes and bring water — there’s a lot of walking but totally worth it if you like history and cool views.

Absolutely amazing tour! Family ranging from 7-70s, the organisation was fantastic from the start, clear meeting point and instructions of the tour. Visiting the Colosseum was absolutely breathtaking! The guided tours are super informative too, giving you a deep insight into Roman culture and architecture.

What’s also great is how easy it is to reach — it’s right in the heart of Rome, surrounded by other amazing sites like the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill.
The good:

Stunning architecture and rich history

Great photo spots from every angle

Informative tours and exhibits

A little not so good:

Can get really crowded, especially during peak season

Overall, the Colosseum is a must-visit even with the crowds, it’s an unforgettable experience that truly captures the spirit of ancient Rome.

Despite the centuries that have passed, the Colosseum retains an incredible charm. The view from the upper level offers a unique perspective of the Roman Forum and the eternal city, a testament to its enduring significance.

As a tip, to avoid long queues, it’s recommended to purchase tickets in advance. Additionally, consider spending some time exploring the Forum and Palatine Hill, which are often included in the Colosseum ticket.

Visiting the Colosseum in Rome was like stepping back in time to one of history's most iconic landmarks. As I approached this massive amphitheater, the sheer scale and grandeur of the structure were breathtaking. Its towering arches and ancient stones, weathered by nearly 2,000 years of history, were a humbling sight to behold.

Once inside, I marveled at the incredible engineering and design that allowed this architectural marvel to host over 50,000 spectators. Walking through the passageways, I imagined the roar of the crowds, the thrill of gladiator battles, and the incredible spectacles that once took place here. The underground tunnels, where animals and gladiators once waited for their moment on the arena floor, were especially fascinating to see.

From the upper levels, the view of Rome was stunning. Looking out over the ancient city, it was easy to appreciate why the Colosseum is considered one of the New Seven Wonders of the World. This experience wasn't just about seeing a famous monument—it was about feeling connected to a piece of world history, imagining the lives of those who came before us, and marveling at the resilience of human craftsmanship.

Visiting the Colosseum was an unforgettable experience that gave me a new appreciation for the incredible history of Rome. I highly recommend it to anyone with a love of history and architecture!
I so enjoy seeing historical sites.  You should pay for a guide.  You will have an opportunity to see more levels with a guide.

Booking your time and visit in advance makes it really easy to enter and enjoy this legendary space easily. Everyone starts taking photos up top immediately so it tends to get crowded at first. Better to keep going until space opens up and then take pictures with fewer people around and better views. Don’t worry, there will be lots of opportunities for great shots! If it’s your first time in Rome, and you only have one day, I recommend just seeing it from the outside and continuing on to see all the other historic sights from outside. If you do enter, you can pay for the audio guide (or a real guide) or get guided tours for free on Spotify or YouTube. You can also book more detailed tours that take you to other parts of the location other than just the standard visit. It would have been even more amazing on a sunny day, but despite the weather, we had a fantastic time snapping countless photos. The Colosseum is truly a must-visit in Rome!

The Colosseum, also known as the Flavian Amphitheatre, is one of the most iconic landmarks in Rome and a symbol of ancient Roman engineering. Built between 70-80 AD, it could hold up to 80,000 spectators who gathered to watch gladiatorial contests, animal hunts, and other public spectacles. Its impressive architecture features a series of arches and columns, showcasing the grandeur of Roman design.

What makes the Colosseum even more fascinating is its history. It was the site of epic battles and dramatic performances, and over the centuries, it has witnessed the rise and fall of empires. Today, visitors can explore its vast interior, where the remains of the arena floor and the underground chambers where gladiators and animals were kept can be seen.

Visiting the Colosseum is not just about seeing a historical site; it’s about immersing yourself in the rich culture and stories that shaped ancient Rome. Whether you're standing in the arena or walking through the arches, you can almost hear the echoes of the past and feel the excitement of the crowds that once filled this magnificent structure. It’s truly an unforgettable experience!
Beautiful by night and also during the day. Metro station is next to colosseum so you can reach this point from around the Rome.

An interesting place to visit. It's better to buy tickets in advance. Keep your passport or any ID with you. There is a security check and a scan before entering. Take only the necessary things with you.

Absolutely breathtaking! The wonderful people, stunning architecture, and incredible views make it an absolute must-visit destination. You won't want to miss this experience!

A breathtaking step into ancient Rome — with a few tips to make it even better

Visiting the Colosseum was one of the most unforgettable experiences of my trip to Rome. The sheer size and presence of this ancient amphitheatre are overwhelming — it truly feels like stepping back in time. Walking through the stands, tunnels, and underground chambers, you can picture the roar of the crowd and the scale of the events that once took place here. The engineering and architecture are absolutely remarkable.

We booked a guided tour with arena and underground access, which I’d highly recommend — it not only allowed us to skip the ticket queue, but added so much context and life to what we were seeing. Our guide brought the history alive in vivid detail.

🕐 Best time to visit:

We arrived just before opening time at 8:30 AM, which made a huge difference. The crowds were minimal, the temperature was comfortable, and we could take our time without feeling rushed. If mornings aren’t possible, late afternoons (after 4 PM) are also quieter — and the light is beautiful for photos.

💡 Tips to avoid queues:
• Buy tickets online in advance — either from the official website or a trusted tour provider. Don’t wait to buy at the gate; the lines can be massive.
• If you can, go with a guided tour — you’ll skip the main queue and access areas that general tickets don’t include, like the arena floor and hypogeum.
• Avoid weekends and aim for Tuesday to Thursday if you’re visiting during high season.
• Don’t forget that your Colosseum ticket also includes Palatine Hill and the Roman Forum — plan for a half-day to see it all.

Loved it. Booked earliest tickets I could and was glad cause it was hot. Also was the only time we’re sure we had pickpockets around us (so watch your bag in line).

The Coliseum, also known as the Flavian Amphitheater, is one of the most iconic landmarks in the world. Located in Rome, Italy, this ancient structure showcases the architectural brilliance of the Roman Empire. It was built around 70-80 AD and could hold over 50,000 spectators for gladiator fights, public events, and dramas.

Even today, the Coliseum stands as a symbol of power, history, and culture. Visiting it feels like stepping back in time. The stone walls, arches, and underground chambers tell stories of both glory and cruelty. It’s a must-visit for history lovers and travelers alike.

Visiting the Colosseum on a guided tour during the day is an unforgettable deep dive into ancient Roman history. The Skip-the-Line access makes a huge difference, allowing visitors to explore the arena floor, underground chambers, and higher levels without wasting time in long lines. The tour extends to the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, offering breathtaking views and rich historical insights into Rome’s political and social past. Every turn reveals architectural marvels and compelling stories, making this an essential experience for history lovers.

At night, the Colosseum transforms into something even more awe-inspiring, illuminated against the dark sky. Walking around with family, the atmosphere is peaceful, almost surreal, with fewer crowds and a more personal connection to its grandeur. Standing near the arches, capturing selfies in the soft glow of the lights, you get a whole new appreciation for its scale and presence. While a night tour inside would be incredible, just taking in its beauty from the outside feels like stepping back in time.

One of the most iconic landmarks in Rome — truly impressive to see in person. The scale, history, and architecture of the Colosseum are awe-inspiring.

Tip: Book tickets in advance to avoid long queues. The combination ticket includes access to the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, which are right next door. After visiting the Colosseum, start with Palatine Hill for a panoramic view, then walk down into the Roman Forum.

Best to visit early in the day, especially in summer. Bring a hat, plenty of water, and sun protection. It gets very hot with little shade.

A must-see if it’s your first time in Rome!

The Colosseum is the largest amphitheatre ever built. It is also the largest standing amphithetre in the world. Emperor Vespasian started the construction of the Collosseum in 72 AD. It was completed in 80 AD. Built of limestone, volcanic rock and concrete. It used to be the arena for public spectacles like animal hunts and gladiatorial fights. As many as 80,000 spectators used to assemble in it at a time. It remains as a renowned symbol of imperial Rome. When you stand before it you will be filled with awe at its grandeur and size.

The Colosseum is absolutely beautiful and an incredible place to visit. It’s massive and really impressive to see in person. Tickets are quite cheap for children and students, which is great. If you come around 7–7:30 in the morning, there will be a big queue, but once it opens you can go inside for free. The ticket also includes the Roman Forum (though not all levels). Definitely a must-see in Rome.

An unforgettable, bucket-list experience in Paris!

Visiting the Louvre was an absolute highlight of my trip to Paris! The sheer scale of the museum is mind-blowing, and the building itself a stunning former royal palace is just as much a masterpiece as the art inside. Looking up at the incredibly ornate, gilded ceilings and walking through the grand galleries felt surreal.

Of course, seeing Leonardo da Vinci’s Mona Lisa in person was an unforgettable moment. Yes, the room is busy, but the line moves quickly and it is completely worth it. Beyond that, the Winged Victory of Samothrace standing tall at the top of the grand staircase and the beautiful Venus de Milo were absolutely breathtaking.

My top tips for anyone visiting:

1. Book your tickets online well in advance to secure your time slot and bypass the massive ticket lines.

2. Wear your most comfortable walking shoes because the museum is massive and you will easily clock in thousands of steps.

3. Pick a few must-see pieces before you go, or you will get easily overwhelmed

It is an absolute 5-star experience that you cannot miss when in Paris. Truly a journey through history and art!

The Louvre is not only an incredible museum but also a very large and impressive area to explore. Even outside the museum itself, you can easily spend half a day walking around the beautiful courtyards, the famous glass pyramid, and the surrounding architecture. The entire complex is stunning and offers many great spots for photos and to simply enjoy the atmosphere of Paris. A must-visit place, even if you don’t plan to go inside the museum.

Are you even in Louvre if you don’t get lost every 5 minutes. Amazing piece of history and helps us learn history.

Even 8 hours in the museum wouldnt be enough to see everything you wanna see. Get ready to walk a lot.

Top picks: Select paintings and exhibits you want to see and book a ticket well in advance. Also reach the place atleast 30 minutes before your admit time.

The museum is absolutely breathtaking, with stunning architecture, beautiful galleries, and an incredible collection of art from around the world. From the iconic Mona Lisa to the magnificent sculptures and historical treasures, every room offered something fascinating to discover.

The museum offered countless opportunities for curiosity, learning, and family discussions about art, history, and culture.

We spent hours exploring and still felt there was more to see. The Louvre is much more than a museum—it is a journey through human creativity, culture, and history. An unforgettable family experience that inspired both adults and children alike. Highly recommended for visitors of all ages and a wonderful destination for families traveling with children!

From world-famous paintings to magnificent sculptures, the museum is filled with invaluable treasures collected from different eras and cultures. It is not just a museum—it is a place where creativity and artistic inspiration come alive. Art lovers, photographers, and curious travelers alike will find endless wonders to explore.

My wife and I recently spent our vacation in Paris after traveling from Italy, and we had the opportunity to visit the iconic Louvre Museum during a very hot day, though we were grateful to find plenty of relief in the cool air provided by the shelter of those thick, ancient stone walls. It must be admitted that the vast majority of this museum’s global fame is owed to a single painting by the Italian genius Leonardo da Vinci, which naturally led us to reflect on the sheer superiority and majestic vastness that our home country of Italy, and specifically Florence, continues to offer to tourists from all over the world.

1. The Louvre is the world’s most visited museum, attracting millions of visitors every year.
2. It was originally built as a fortress in the late 12th century before becoming a royal palace.
3. The museum officially opened to the public in 1793 during the French Revolution.
4. The glass pyramid entrance was designed by architect I. M. Pei and opened in 1989. At first, many Parisians hated it. Today it’s one of the symbols of Paris.
5. The Louvre has more than 35,000 artworks on display, but its total collection is far larger.
6. The most famous artwork inside is the Mona Lisa by Leonardo da Vinci.
7. Other must-see masterpieces include the Venus de Milo and the Winged Victory of Samothrace.
8. The museum is so large that if you spent just 30 seconds looking at every object in its collection, it would take months to see everything.
9. During World War II, many artworks were secretly evacuated from the Louvre to protect them from Nazi seizure.
10. The Louvre sits beside the Seine River in the center of Paris and is connected to the historic Tuileries Garden, making the entire area feel like one giant open-air museum.
A must go tourist attraction. See the real Mona Lisa painting & fine arts. If the weather is good. Stay around the area and have a picnic over wine or champagne.

Plan a whole day. For those who are there only for the first time: the Pyramids are only the entrance, all the buildings surounding you are the actual museum. The Louvre: Hidden Gems & Fast-Walking Strategy
The Louvre requires a clear strategy. On October 5, 2023, my wife and I explored as "fast walkers," skipping crowded sections to focus on truly irreplaceable works. We tried to enter the museum earlier than our slot but were not successful; stick strictly to your time.
* 3:20 PM [Photos 3 & 4]: Started with a lovely, sweeping coastal view and took a photo of Alexandre-Hyacinthe Dunouy's View of Naples from Capodimonte. Right next to it hung Antonio Joli's The Departure of Charles III from Naples. Seeing all the intricate sailboats leaving the harbor, my instincts immediately told me to get a photo of this!
* 3:29 PM – 3:34 PM [Photo 5]: Made a fast 11-minute transition from the quiet Naples paintings to the Apollo Gallery. A stunning experience of "shining gold." The Crown of Louis XV, with its diamonds and rubies, is a dazzling must-see.
* 3:53 PM – 3:58 PM [Photos 13, 14, & 15]: Arrived at the Mona Lisa at 3:53 PM. My wife had better luck than most people, capturing a pristine shot with no crowds on the frame. Right behind us, the massive The Wedding at Cana was taken showing the full room crowds finishing at 3:58 PM.
* 4:03 PM [Photo 16]: Moving quickly ahead, I found Parmigianino’s famous portrait, Antea. We successfully tracked this masterpiece down by following Ads posted outside the museum.
* 4:08 PM [Photo 17]: Finished with the 18th-century elegance of Thomas Gainsborough's Lady Alston, where I loved the fine details of her blue silk skirt against the trees.
💡 Tip: Don't try to see everything. Move fast between the main wings to maximize your time. If you don’t want to buy a ticket, touring outside around the Cour Napoléon to see the historic palace architecture is a highly valuable, free alternative.

This was a must on our list of activities for our first trip to Paris and it didn't disappoint. There's so much here! We did the Mona Lisa and the Venus de Milo but enjoyed all the other galleries as well.

We still had to queue but it was much shorter than the queue outside and it was underground with air conditioning. I'd also recommend turning up early for your slot - we arrived (mostly) on time and the queue was already long. It was a Bank Holiday so peak time!

Easily one of the best museums in the world and a must-see, but you need a strategy! First, skip the food and coffee inside—it's overpriced, mediocre, and there’s nowhere to sit. Second, avoid the ground floor bathrooms unless you want to wait 20 minutes in line. Most importantly, be clever and ignore the "Start Here" sign, otherwise you'll walk for hours and be too exhausted to enjoy the best parts. The Louvre Museum is recognized as part of a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1991. This heritage area extends along the Seine river from the Louvre to the Eiffel Tower and includes other iconic monuments like Notre-Dame Cathedral and the Place de la Concorde. The museum typically draws millions of visitors annually, making it one of the most-visited museums in the world. The Louvre is the world's largest art museum and a historic monument, originally built as a fortress in the 12th century. The museum houses famous masterpieces such as the Mona Lisa of Leonardo Da Vinci and the Venus de Milo.
The palace complex is situated along the Right Bank of the Seine River, adjacent to the Tuileries Garden. Underneath the pyramid is a large lobby that provides access to the museum's various wings, along with ticketing and information desks. The museum, which was originally built as a fortress in the 12th century and later served as a royal palace, has been open to the public since 1793.

The Louvre Museum is an absolute masterpiece and a total bucket-list experience when you are in Paris! As the world's largest art museum, it is housed in a stunning historic palace that blends classic architecture with the iconic modern glass pyramid out front. Inside, it is a massive treasure trove where you can see legendary pieces like the Mona Lisa and the Venus de Milo up close. It is incredibly vast, so it is best to pick a few wings you are most excited about rather than trying to see it all in one day.
