5. Even the placards Don't match the elevator! (See video) Seriously?
6. Barely any seats in the main hall for thousands of museum guests.
7. This is like IKEA where you need to walk an hour to exit the building. Even staff didn't know how to get us out! Ridiculous
They need to have special maps for people to show what elevators are out and how to exit the floors!! Just stupid!

Although the museum itself is beautiful and the artworks are breathtaking, unfortunately, I rate the Louvre as an institution poorly. The reserved entry times to the museum are only nominal; on the day I visited, part of the exhibition was closed due to a strike in Paris. However, if someone travels from the other side of the world to see an exhibition, they should have the opportunity to do so, and if not, some form of compensation should be offered. Unfortunately, according to the Louvre staff, no such compensation is available and it is against their policy. Even when trying to claim compensation, you won’t hear a simple apology for the situation and inconvenience caused. Additionally, on the day of my visit, the audioguides were unavailable due to modernization work. However, when I booked in June, this situation was not foreseeable, and again, no monetary compensation was given. To sum up, the museum itself is beautiful and worth visiting, but the staff rely heavily on the museum’s reputation and show little interest in making the visit more pleasant and easier for visitors, assuming that tourist numbers will remain high despite the level of service.

It was a miserable experience.  Too many people,  no ventilation,  I'm  surprised  no one fainted.  Have a plan in case you get separated from your family or group because this place is a a maze. Most of the time was spent trying to find an AC vent that I missed  most of the art.  The glass dome was cool but after you pass the that,  it's mayhem inside. Warning.

Man ist in der Präsentation von weltbekannter Kunst irgendwann in der Zeit stehen geblieben. Schlechte bis gar keine Informationen an den Exponaten und Gemälden. Räume sind schlecht ausgewiesen. Als Museum einfach wirklich schlecht. Viele andere Galerien und Museen dieser Welt haben viel besser verstanden ihr Kunst zu präsentieren.

This is the second time I've come to this museum. Before, I came in the winter and the experience was a thousand times better. From a user's perspective, analyzing the commercial side of the enterprise, the museum is an embarrassment. Unlike spaces like the British Museum (also built from the European colonial heritage), we have to pay €22 to get in, but the infrastructure is terrible (not to mention that if you leave the building, you can't go back. So, you have just one time-slot to arrive and then, stay there until your body can endure).
The bathrooms are the worst part. Besides being scarce for the number of people who circulate, there are 30-minute lines to get in, and they are dirty, without toilet paper, and without soap to wash your hands. For one of the most famous museums in the world, the least you expect is an intense level of organization to ensure the safety and comfort of visitors. There are no water fountains, and the cafés are extremely expensive - even comparing it to the average prices on champs elisees.
Really, it's an infinite money-making machine in exchange for super-inflated tourism. A shame considering the cultural importance of the works on display.

So, so busy. With large numbers of people taking ages to take selfies at the front of the Mona Lisa queue, our experience was severely marred by many rude, selfish tourists. The building is amazing with some of the best artwork in the world but I would not visit again unless numbers were limited or at a much quieter time of year.

The place is great and beautiful from the outside but there's way toooo much paintings there.. Probably 70% of it or even more in my opinion.
If You are looking for big exhibition of Greek, Egyptian or Roman stuff You could be disapointed. And many things have no info in english about it so good luck in reading that if You don't know french.

Beautiful museum but incredibly crowded. Lots of unique art pieces and art relics but if I’m being totally honest it’s a little boring and the British Museum in London absolutely blows the Louvre out of the water. Also there is no air conditioning so it’s really hot and muggy. I can appreciate the beautiful art but not for me.

The Louvre has an amazing collection of art. However, the museum is very chaotic and it’s hard to keep track of the rooms that you’ve seen. The touring group was too big and the audio quality was bad. Really loud staff at the checkin

Amazing artwork, but organization and layout is terrible. Halls to nowhere and stairs up then stairs down. They couldn’t make a decent layout or map in 100 years? Good luck finding the exit or bathrooms as well.

World class museum. I could spend days here.

However, getting into the museum was difficult. I pre-purchased tickets online with designated entrance time and ended up waiting an hour to enter. There were tons of groups trying to cut into the line to avoid waiting (which means everyone is waiting longer). I’d like to see more staff managing the long queue and more security checkpoints/machines to reduce the extreme bottleneck.

This may be one of the most disorganized and illogically designed museums in the world. Apparently, you need different tickets for different sections, but this isn’t clearly stated anywhere on the website. The signage is abysmal, and the layout feels like a labyrinth. You have to show your ticket five or six times just to get in—and again when entering various sections of the museum.

Despite buying timed tickets in advance, we still had to wait in a line for over 30 minutes just to get inside. There are lines everywhere, for everything. Even during the off-season, it’s so crowded that you can’t really stop and appreciate the art.

While I understand we’re in France and French is the primary language, the crowd is international. Some signs and descriptions are multilingual, but many are only in French, which is frustrating.

It’s also very hot inside. Clearly, the ventilation system can’t keep up with the sheer number of visitors.

I’d say avoid it—but that’s hardly an option, considering the one-of-a-kind artwork housed here. Just be prepared to be frustrated. And despite the Mona Lisa being the main attraction, its location isn’t obvious or well-marked. Honestly, I expected better.

Really it worth the time or money. Even with express access it took forever to get in, they let way to many people in at one time and the entire experience is a hot, smelly rabbit warren with the occasional interesting piece. Don’t waste your time or money, I can’t get mine back.

Me and my partner were unaware that we had to arrive 30 minutes before your booking (although we did so anyways). Even so, there was a long queue for at least an hour or so to pass a security check before entering. The room with Mona Lisa is overcrowded and you can only see her from afar. The painting opposite Mona Lisa of the Last Supper is some impressive work that doesn't receive similar attention!

It was cool but so busy. It felt like in a can of sardines. If I redid my first trip to Paris, I would skip the Louvre. Going here is probably the most touristy thing you can do in Paris, but you’re not really able to appreciate it because of how crowded it is. There are plenty of other museums in Paris to visit that are amazing but won’t have you feeling like you’re in a mosh pit at a rock concert where you just get pushed around by everyone because it’s so crowded.

Very nice place!
toilets are very dirty!
AND THE MORE IMPORTANT THEY DON'T USE ENGLISH LANGUAGE ON THE 90% OF EXHIBITS

Wished we could have got in. We did all our research ahead of time on tickets to all the major museums. Come to find out the Monday we got here the Louvre had just switched their policy of only selling so many tickets and then it is closed. It even made the news that they did not announce it properly ahead of time. So now this is the only major museum we did not get to see and through a wrench in to our plans. Just unfortunate when you take a once in a lifetime trip and you cannot get into the Louvre and Notre Dame had caught fire a few months earlier. Oh well...

Although we had booked tickets and went to low season dates, we had to wait 1 hour to get in the museum. Many collections have displays written only in French.. How are non-French speaking tourists supposed to read and understand what they see..?
Also most displays are "borrowed" from other countries and civilizations..
They should at least write the description in English and also in the language from where they "borrowed" the item..
One very large section of the museum displays Greek art and sculpture..Yet at the central information desk they didn't have the courtesy to put a "welcome" in Greek language.

I was very disappointed with the Louvre. It was extremely crowded, the audio guide was neat on the 3DS, but it was too wordy. Each description would last 5 minutes. There were massive amounts of people everywhere, and a 45 minute wait to see the Mona Lisa for 30 seconds. I'm glad I went, but I don't need to go back.

Too crowded. No time to appreciate anything.
Too much to see.
Like any other museum, no point to visit if you want to appreciate details. It should be visited out of public timetable.

*Audio Guide Review*
The audio guide in the Louvre is probably one of the worst I've had in the world.
First you need to realize that to get the guide you need to buy tickets but the ticket office and the guide stand are in different locations. So to understand this you first need to stand in one line to get this info, then in another to buy the tickets, and after that get back in the first line to get the device.

Frustrating.

After I've got the device I was excited to finally get some insights into the fine works but quickly realized the device was broken...

Back to the line again, changed the device,it finally worked! So I went to the exhibition.

Operating it wasn't as straightforward as I expected so I had to spend some time to actually learn how to operate it.

After the learnings were finishing, I was finally ready to listen.

In the exhibitions there were a number of works I was specifically interested in so I went there. But when I finally got to the works the final realization was that THERE WERE NO RECORDINGS OR INFORMATION ABOUT THE SPECIFIC WORKS! And I'm not saying just about the one that was so interesting to me and the one I bought the guide for - there were no recordings for specific works at all! When you type a number of works there was nothing, not just for a couple - for any number whatsoever. Zero!

What the guide has is a coupe minutes explanation about a room and maybe a work or two in there and that's it. It's very shallow, lacking depth and nuances I was looking for which resulted in a bad experience.

So from the purchasing, to operating, to listening - it was of a very poor quality I couldn't expect from the biggest and most known museum in the world.
No problem with the Lourve per se but had to queue for 30 mins to pay for audio guide, followed by another 30 mins to then collect said audio guide. There must be a better way.

The most disappointing tourist spot in Paris. Overcrowded, toilets are dirty, it felt like a scavenger hunt/labirinth. Very disappointing, would not recommend to anyone, just look up the pictures online.

It is amazing how organised it looks, but how terrible it is organised with unclear waitinglines that will have you stand in the sun for at least an hour. The other choice is to wait just as long in a shoppingmall, where the air feels dense.

The wait was almost 2 hours to get inside today because of the strike.   One we finally did get inside, the whole top floor was closed and most of the floor below it was also closed.   If we were told that in line, then I probably would have just left.  The bathrooms are dirty, the staff are not helpful, and the signage is very poor, no matter what language you speak.   The ONLY reason I rated it even a 2 was that the artwork we DID see was beautiful.  This is over hyped and may not be worth going inside.

Beautiful museum and beautiful paintings.

Horribly difficult website, specially, for a tourist to book anything, unhelpful staff, extremely unorganized museum with a useless map for a visitor, dirty and smelly bathrooms, etc.

Extremely easy to get lost as there are ways to everywhere! How hard is it to print a simple walk though map for a visitor from the most busiest museum in Paris?

Very poor organization and navigation. The queues are extremely long — I had a ticket for 10:00 but was only able to enter around 11:00. It’s impossible to get close to some of the paintings. The artworks are displayed in a chaotic way, without clear logic or flow. The room with the Mona Lisa is overcrowded, making it impossible to calmly view the masterpieces by Veronese and Titian nearby. The atmosphere is hectic, and there aren’t enough staff members to assist visitors. The restrooms are old and not properly cleaned.
Exceptionally long waiting time, despite having a booking slot. Not easy to navigate and no staff to direct us to the Mona Lisa. Very tiring with young children. But, my older daughter enjoyed it.

Air conditioning is the worst. I never expected such bad air quality in what should be a world destination. High entry cost. Crowded. Long entry queue. Horrible guidance. Bad lighting. Low maintenance. Lack of food or water. No proper wall stamps.

The Louvre was a miserable experience this time.  It seems poorly managed,  many of the most famous paintings are dirty, discolored,  and badly in need of restoration.  There are at least 100 people crowding,  pushing and elbowing in front of the Mona Lisa, with one bored guard behind the barrier. It is too warm in the galleries,  it's bad for the paintings.  Many paintings are covered with glass, which is understandable, due to indifference to restoration,  but it makes them hard to see with the glare. I spent a lot of time in the Louvre as an art student, and it is just sad now.  I gave 2 stars, because well, it's the Louvre.

The entrance to the museum is haotic, we have booked time entering,  but there is no sign, all the people are waiting in the lines, and they have no information in what line should they wait. We have waiting in one line, after that in another, very haotic and nervous situation. Other wise the Museum is great, but if you want to see closer Mona Lisa, there is no chance is to crowd, there people make selfies like they are on the concert, maybe some waiting line is solution. Something what is woth to see is Egyptian exhibition.

The Egyptian section is closed on Thursday and Friday, no idea why, which is a bummer since I went just to see that. (didn't see anything infos about that on the website)
Even though they have plans of the museum I found it very difficult to go around, it's just not clear. The indications are not clear at all.
Super hard to find someone from the museum to help if you have any questions concerning directions. And nobody at the point of information most of the time. The only way to get in is to pay in advance on the internet you can't just go there and buy a ticket, which I think is a mistake (not everyone is able to have access on the internet)
If you love the museum of natural history then you should only like the louvre and always an entrance fee but in America it’s a donation

Queue was crazy long to enter the lourve. We didnt not plan to enter. please book tickets in advance. Cant imagine the crowd inside. But was pretty crowded with lotsa tourist. We took uber there and the driver did not send us inside. I guess only taxi can enter the area. Please take note

The charm and history of viewing art within the Lourve is lost a little by the hordes of tourists that stampede through it everyday.
Tourists treat this place like an amusement park, constantly taking photos of every piece of art then moving (rushing) on to the next.
The audio guide enhanced the experience but the crowds detracted from my experience when I was simply attempting to marvel at the art on display.

28/08/2925 pm. Unfortunately, not a good experience just getting into the gallery, the staff were really unpleasant and rude particularly one officious lady.
The ticket assistance office were helpful and attempted to help us after buying a scam joint ticket for the museum and boat trip .
BE CAREFUL ABOUT SCAM TICKET SALES ONLINE.
It is horrible paying twice and worse if the staff like this particular suited lady are mean to you . She should realise she is privileged to work with beautiful aren’t works. She needs replacing. It only takes one to spoil the experience and she was the person who did.

Really bad crowd management, seems like there are not enough staff or they want to have masses of people waiting here and there. They sell way too many tickets for their capacity to service them well. In no way seeing the most famous pieces can be a pleasant experience with the way the crowd is controlled. And toilets are a horror to use, few in number, long lines, and so filthy and by most of the world’s standards out of order really.

Apesar da beleza do lugar é uma desorganização total e falta de informação. Mesmo comprando ingresso com horário marcado você fica embaixo de um sol escaldante esperando para entrar. Os banheiros tem longas filas, muito sujos. A acessibilidade não existe. Muitas escadas e os poucos elevadores desativados. Impossível se chegar ao quadro da Monalisa. As pessoas se aglomeram em frente ao quadro e não saem. Deveria ter um caminho feito por organizador de fila para que se pudesse passar em frente ao mesmo.

The experience at the museum is nice, but let’s be honest - most people come to see the Mona Lisa. And that’s where the horror show begins. The museum staff is completely disorganized when it comes to letting people take pictures; everyone ends up packed together, trying to get a decent photo. And once you step out of the tape they put up to separate people from the painting, they start shouting and sticking their arms in front of your phone so you can’t take more pictures. In other words, take your photos while everyone’s pushing you - otherwise, too bad for you. Especially if you’re short, like me. We travel from another country, spend money, only for the staff to be rude simply because they can’t manage the crowd properly. Zero stars for the entire team.

The Louvre itself is fine, access to toilets in the shops area is ridiculous. You have to pay to use the toilets. This would be fine if the toilets were kept in good condition as a result, but they are not - they are disgusting.

Terrible experience but great art
Booking was for 11:30, we arrived at 10. Was told we can’t queue until 11. The queue for security was so long we didn’t get in until 12:15 even though we wanted to join earlier.

We applied for 11:30 entrance time, not a time to enter the line which is actually what they offer.
Once inside you once again queue to enter the galleries.

Staff are rude and say nothing to people cutting the line/queue.

Art is good but if this was any other gallery it would have no star rating the the art is amazing.

Beautiful place but too crowded and they keep coming to you and ask you to buy souvenirs
And the entrance smells bad i was disappointed

Huge, but poorly signed. I wish they were better organised in crowd control over the Mona Lisa painting. I know this is supposedly a world class museum, but compared to the rest, it is a very poorly managed one.

The place is not managed well. There are lots of barriers to get to the museum and you get tired before you start visiting the museum. First you have to stay in a 1 hour line just to get in. Then you have difficulty finding your way in the museum too. There are not enough signs in the building so you have to find a person and to ask for information.
There are lots of stairs and very limited elevators, so if you have kids in trolley, you have to wait in the long lines if the elevators too.
The other problem is that almost all the explanations of the arts and pieces are only in french language. So you probably will not understand them unless you know french or you have enough patience to translate everything in another language.
Very disappointed that the staff had a strike resulting in this closing. Our tour was cancelled as a result.

I can't recommend going for one reason and one reason only . I have never seen such Queues and Inthe baking heat.. even when you got inside it was queued out everywhere . I was so looking forward to it but after an hour I was exhausted and left . .

Arrived 45 minutes before my pre-booked time yet still had to queue for over 90 minutes to enter. (Thursday 27th March 2025, mid-morning).

Inside it is far too busy and I found it poorly signed so even with the map it was difficult to navigate and find the rooms that interested me.

Obviously it’s an incredible collection and amazing building but it cannot cope with the volume of visitors which ruined the experience. They need to limit tickets to be less than current allocation which was too crowded to properly appreciate anything. If you cannot enter at your allocated time then there is an issue. Was very disappointed by this poor management.

Dead of winter early morning only, it is an astonishing collection. Any other time of year: Crowds are grotesque. Booking arrangement is ludicrous, no way to avoid multi hour wait, even on a weekday. Doable in small doses off season, wish I could live there for a year to get a better feel of and appreciation for the place!

While the building and art are breathtaking and among some of the greatest in the world there were some things that made my visit unenjoyable. Firstly I traveled almost 6,000 kilometers to specifically see the middle eastern and Levantine collection and NOWHERE on the site does it mention that the gallery is closed on Mondays. I could not come back another day as I was leaving on Wednesday and I was truly heartbroken because art from my culture is not widely represented and I really wanted to see them. I wouldve booked another day had I known Secondly the crowds were a logistical nightmare, it was confusing and disorganized to go from section to section with hoards of tourists elbowing and cutting in. There needs to be a greater uniformity in visual organization. I could not enjoy my time in one of the supposed greatest museums because of these logistical nightmares. They should learn from the Met an equally famous museum, which is always organized and never feels like a madhouse.
They let more people in than they should, it's all like cows queuing at the slaughterhouse, absokutely unenjoyable

This is not about the art but about the organisation..Although we had booked tickets we queued for 45 minutes in a stuffy hall. Why? Because the Louvre had provided only ONE security device at each entrance. Given it costs 22 euros to go in and the Louvre receives thousands of visitors every day this suggests a lack of concern and respect for visitors which I find very disappointing.

Massive crowds ruin the experience. Might be because I went at 1pm on a Monday, maybe earlier or later in the afternoon is better. Toilets are also awful! Smelly, out of use or with really long lines. However, staff are wonderful and helpful. Xx

Very disappointed with the staff. They were extremely rude and even shouted at my children simply because they didn’t hear the order number. To make matters worse, the number was called out in French — which we don’t speak as tourists — so of course my children didn’t understand. Instead of showing patience, the staff mocked us by making faces and gestures as if we were deaf. Totally unacceptable behavior!

I wanted to love the Louvre as this was my first time here... I never wanted to leave a place so quick in my life. The architecture and the collections are outstanding, but it was hard to appreciate them with the scale of the massive crowds. Better crowd control is desperately needed, especially around the Mona Lisa.

Quite disappointed- if you have specific masterpieces you want to see, beware of that the mgmt closes multiple areas at once, MORE than what they officially publish ! Status on Jun 16th is: Napoleon Chambers CLOSED, French Painting in Sully galleries CLOSED, Ancient Egypt CLOSED, European sculpture CLOSED, Africa/Oceania/Asia CLOSED. It’s much more than what they posted!

Amazing collection of art but really a bad experience overall. Insanely packed with massive crowds of tourists. Expect theme park crowds and lines - you will not enter until well after your scheduled ticket time and you won’t be able to walk freely anywhere inside. Spent more time in lines and claustrophobically crowded rooms than looking at art. Felt like a wasted morning but perhaps cool if you like standing around in lines and crowds all day, unable to even view a single painting in peace and left disappointed.
A lot of the exhibitions have french only description. that is really disrespectful for all museums and the tourists. too bad because it might have been educating..

Place is well maintained but the real turn off is that it's difficult to navigate within. Route maps are the biggest failure here. Have tried asking more than 10 different groups as I was not able to navigate, but all of them had the same problem navigating.

So if route maps and directions are improved , it's a good place to visit to see & witness the European art.
Very beautiful collections. But it's oxymoron to be in the biggest museum in France, and there aren't anything from the Celts, the real ethnic culture of the country

I just was impressed with the level of disorganization they have there, lots of rude people working there with not even the minimum sense of work at a touristic place. Tickets are expensive and even whit the reservation you have to wait at least half an hour or more to get in so I don’t see the point of reservation. Inside the place it’s huge but full with caos and seems that nobody cares for it. Very nice building, architecture and art it’s a shame that you cannot appreciate it with at least a drop of order. I think visiting here is more for the checklist than other things.

Amazing museum, but we were not allowed to visit the whole part of Napoleon’s apartments because of some “private event” that was taking place. Think of someone who comes from the other side of the world and hears this…

While the Louvre holds an extraordinary collection of art and artifacts from around the world, the visitor experience could be significantly improved. One major issue is the lack of English translations for most exhibit descriptions, which makes it difficult for non-French-speaking visitors to fully appreciate the historical context. Additionally, the museum map is confusing and the layout makes navigation challenging. It’s easy to get lost, and locating specific room numbers often turns into a frustrating task. For such a world-renowned institution, more accessible signage and multilingual support would greatly enhance the overall experience.
Waaaaay too busy and noisy. No time or space to admire anything and half of the paintings rooms were closed. Wouldn't visit again!

A beautiful collection, but much of the information was inaccessible as a non-French speaker. I don't mind using translation technology, but I was disappointed that a museum that draws people in from all over the world hardly translated any of the artifact information and history of the antiquities it holds. Additionally, regarding access needs, there was not adequate seating or toilets for the number of people in the museum.

Massively overated.

Viewing the Mona Lisa is a bun fight.. I tried but couldn't stand because of disability, so security let me to the front. I wasn't even there 30 seconds when a rude American shouted insults (because we had a close view... he didn't). I left that area so upset. Staff hardly reacted.

Cafe area filthy and not cleaned.
Too many people. There were lines to use the restrooms. The exhibits were nice but they need to manage the human crush at the Mona Lisa gallery better.

We got to the line, crowded like your going to see Sabrina Carpenter. Went to see the Mona Lisa, oh boy. Crowded. Literally so crowded that people are just pushing and bumping into you for no reason. GROWN ADULTS. FIX THE WAY PEOPLE GET INTO THE MONA LISA. LIKE GROWN ADULTS.

The Louvre is a magnificent museum. 5 star museum. But when visited with a pram/stroller, it is at best a 2 star experience.

Getting in was fine, easy, lulls you into a false sense of security. We were helped at the front of the line, got through security with minimal problems. Then it began. The hunt for access.

The Louvre is a labyrinth of stairs, blind corners, dead ends and inadequate signage. The elevators are poorly marked, possibly to deter use from people who can use the stairs. This doesn’t entirely work.

A lot of the elevators were out of commission, at least 5 that we tried to use throughout the day. Of course you don’t find this out until you actually find the elevator.

You encounter stairs, small flights or with sharp turns. You’re faced with a choice - carry the pram or hope to find a working elevator. After being denied access to a series of rooms with Egyptian antiquities on floor 0 (patronisingly being told those rooms were always closed on Fridays as if we should have known) we were directed to go back the way we’d come, up a floor, across a series of rooms we’d seen and to descend using elevator C to see the Venus De Milo. Of course elevator C was out of order. After trying multiple other avenues, one of our party carried the pram up and down enough stairs to result in tears.

It also took us half an hour, four lifts and one escalator with a sign stating prams should not be used on it to find our way back to level -2 and the exit.

Clearer signage, multiple references to room numbers instead of the single one you have to hunt for, more working elevators, maps that accurately depict locations of change rooms (at one point we were looking at a paper map and a map on the wall and both showed the change room near room 231 in different places), more helpful informed staff, all these things would make the Louvre a much less frustrating place to visit with a pram.

Good luck to anyone else who tries - you’ll need it!

Very large and diverse collection of paintings, sculptures, archeological stuff etc. Nice to see, unfortunately I can't recommend the visit to non French speakers. 90% of all exhibit descriptions are in French only. I don't understand why such a large and famous museum would do this

Possibly the worst organisation I've ever seen.
We were sent to 3 different queues even with a pre booked ticket and after asking staff if we are in the right one. While waiting, we could see that it was actually faster to opt for the queue without a ticket.
Once in the museum, directions are extremely unclear and there is no route to follow to pass through all rooms logically.
The maps are not helping either with more than half the room numbers missing and also (critical connecting) sections closed off, not indicated on the maps.
Cafés and restaurants are hard to find and the quality of the food is far below average, not deserving of a location like the louvre. The burger was pre baked and it was therefore not possible to take the cheese off for us. Food at McDonald's is more freshly prepared.

All in all, we were very disappointed

The place is always crowded and the line is huge. You better buy the tickets in advance. For many years and many museums, palaces, castles, it is absolutely the worst audio-guide I've ever seen. Not intuitive at all, not logical and also boring, insipid.

Honestly the worst museum experience I've ever had. Overcrowded, stuffy and warm (even in October!), signs are not helpful at all, and challenging to find anyone working there to ask questions. Saw the Mona Lisa, which was amazing, but not a good museum experience.

The art and history are special, but the experience is quite poor. No clear direction on where to go when arriving, and a lack of resources inside. Seeing Mona Lisa was a horrific experience that ruined the visit. There must be a better way to accommodate visitors than shoving them in a room and letting them elbow each other for 30 minutes. Disappointing, to say the least.
Very busy, very crowded, no great maps, no air conditioning. There was over an hour wait in a massive crowd to see Mona Lisa. Over a hr wait to enter with pre ordered tickets

So overcrowded. And even though its the most visited museum in the world and making insane amounts of money, the building is being poorly taken care of. Bathrooms were broken in several ways and dirty, a handrail was broken in one of the stairwells etc. Also had to wait for over 20 min to get in even though we had pre-booked tickets.

The Louvre is a wonderful repository of priceless works of art. However it is simply too busy to stop and examine any. Directions are limited and it is a huge complex to find the aspects you want to see. I'd go the Musée D'Orsay

Way too crowded, had to stay in line even for the escalator and most of the personnel was really cranky. If you really want to see La Joconde, go for it, but it might cost your lige, since people get agressive while waiting in line.

We booked with a timeslot, but still we had to queue in a dedicated line for our timeslot for 45 minutes. Once inside a queue of 30 minutes to use the toilet, then a queue of 20 minutes to actually enter the museum itself.
The museum and art on its own is amazing, but the visiting experience is awful.

Tanta attesa, tanta spesa e la grande delusione..forse giornata sbagliata. Ticket x 12, ci presentiamo lì e alle 10.30 dovevano ancora entrare le file delle 9.00 e successive, un ritardo di un' ora e mezza .. ma nessuno avvisa o chiede scusa .. se non sei tu a chiedere nessuno dice nulla.. totale disorganizzazione.. peccato che tu resti lì al sole x oltre tre ore .. e x fortuna non era il caldo dei giorni scorsi.. comprendo il disguido .. ma io ho pagato tu mi devi avvisare e dare la possibilità di riprogrammare la visita o rimborsare il biglietto.. noi ci si puoi sempre approfittare del turista.

I’m not exactly sure why there are so many amazing reviews of this place…maybe we had a very unique negative experience so take that as you will.

I will totally admit that it has one of the most amazing collections of art and historical pieces in the world but the management of the entry process is an absolute joke. The site says you have to choose a time to enter and can get in within 30 minutes of your time slot or up to 30 minutes after. We got to the Carousel Du Louvre entrance at 9:10 and joined a very long line thinking we wouldn’t be to Security for at least 30 minutes. At 9:20, while in line, a woman was checking tickets and told us we had to leave the line and to respect our time slot - okay, fine. We got out of line for 10 minutes and then joined at the back again. We then proceeded to stand (and I do mean stand, not moving) for over an hour and a half. I started to get upset/worried around 10:15 because the site says you won’t be let in 30 minutes past your time slot. We, of course, waited in line anyway. When we finally got to security, sent our bag through the scanner and each of us through the metal detector (that went off on everyone because you don’t have to remove phones or jackets, etc) and then went on our way in less than 10 seconds each. No idea why it was stopped dead for so long but for a Monday morning I would think they should have been able to get everyone through quickly, especially considering they don’t actually search anyone or anything prior to entering. Once we got through that, we walked straight to the locations that meant the most to us due to our limited time left at the museum. At the entrance to the Sully wing, they scanned our tickets but not once did the threat of not being admitted after our selected time slot expired come up. They shouldn’t even offer time slots.

Anyway, we got to see the things we came to see, mainly the Sully wing, but I likely wouldn’t go again. We’ve spent the last week and a half going to museums in London first and now Paris and this was the most unorganized experience we’ve had. For a museum that has been open for so long, they need to work on their infrastructure a considerable amount.

+2 stars for the amazing collection, -3 for the way the museum is operated.
So disappointed.  Too crowded and it's really just too big to enjoy any of it. If u must go get tix in advance and use under ground entrance.

How sad was this experience??? Think of a trip to "the Mall" with priceless art and antiquities. AND when you cannot tolerate the noise and crowds any longer you look to escape and you find an ACTUAL Mall with a Foodcourt. AWFUL!

Il museo merita assolutamente una visita, ma trovo assurdo che, pur essendo il più visitato al mondo, sia spesso privo di audioguide. Le mappe sono ben dettagliate, ma entrando nelle sale capita di non trovare i numeri identificativi corrispondenti, creando confusione. Inoltre, la Gioconda, il dipinto più famoso al mondo, meriterebbe una collocazione diversa: lo spazio ridotto rispetto all’enorme affluenza rende l’esperienza poco piacevole.

The museum is definitely worth a visit, but I find it quite absurd that, despite being the most visited in the world, it often lacks audioguides. The maps are detailed, yet once inside the rooms it’s not always easy to find the corresponding identifying numbers, which creates confusion. Moreover, the Mona Lisa, the most famous painting in the world, deserves a different placement: the limited space compared to the huge crowds makes the experience less enjoyable.

Negative review solely for the Sep 2024 experience of trying to get in the door. We had a timed ticket for Wednesday 11am. We arrived at 10:45am, waited in line at the Carrousel entrance until 11:30am and realized it'd be another hour or more in line before we could even get in the door. So we gave up.

Ticket taking at the Louvre has always been a problem but the current system of timed tickets plus a single security screening station creates an impossible situation in the mornings.

It's the Louvre, it's one of the world's best collections of art. It's huge and has a lot of amazing things in it, both famous and not. It deserves 6 stars. But selling non-refundable timed tickets and then making visitors wait well over an hour to get inside is unacceptable.

(The happy conclusion: we came back around 3pm and found a friendly person at the group tickets entrance where there was no line. We explained our tale of woe and she waved us right in. That worked for us but not for the hundreds of other people.)

It's a wonderful museum, full of the most important pieces of art. However, it's very difficult to not get lost in it and to understand how to go from one room to another. Many rooms have no number or it's hard to find. Additionally, the museum closes at 18:00 but at 17:30 many rooms were already closed and the workers started to tell us to leave in a very impolite way. Last but not least, if you need the toilet you should pay 2 euros.

The Mona Lisa is absolute chaos, and it's super embarrassing that an international museum like the Louvre can't manage to set up a proper queue. Hundreds of visitors are waiting in front of the Mona Lisa, pushing each other around while some people with VIP passes are pausing in front of the barrier. Only when these VIPs are not there can the hundred people fight over who gets to take a photo in front. Moreover, it's a joke that some people in the front row take photos for five minutes. Normally, the museum should take care of an orderly entrance and exit in the waiting area of the Mona Lisa so that everyone has about 20 seconds and then moves on. Otherwise, the Louvre is quite beautiful and recommendable. But the situation with the Mona Lisa is really a disgrace.

Didn't go in
A rude woman security outside kicked everyone out -no explanation why...
The previous day was closed due to a film...ridiculous
Not worthy visiting inside....
Confusing, crowded, and the selection of art is disappointing. And by god it is hot in there. The Musée d’Orsay is far better. I did enjoy the gift shop, though.

It is almost inspiring that with the riches, art, and history at their disposal, they have made such a bad museum, in one of the most beautiful Palaces in Europe.

The staff is super inhospitable, rude and unhelpful.

The layout is confusing and almost disrespectful in the way it doesn't map the pamflet. The map can lead you one way for 20+ minutes, only for you to realize that that route is closed, and you have to make a half an hour detour. All through a huge mass if people.

The art itself is just bland, there are some jewels though. The Palace is wonderful.

Go visit an art gallery instead, and then go to a nice dinner/lunch for the money you'd spend in Louvre.

Notice how I don't recommend buying tickets in advance, I recommend not buying them at all.
Only given 2 stars as we didn't get in. It was a very wet day and  the queue for tickets was endless. Plan this into your itinerary and purchasing tickets in advance is a must.

The line was absolutely ridiculous on a Wednesday morning. I'm not really an art person, so I didn't think the line was worth it and I just enjoyed the Louvre's architecture from the outside. Beautiful building with many interesting statues. The glass pyramid seems super out of place here, but people seem to like it.

I bet it's beautiful in there.
But we couldn't even get in. We had our tickets but were constantly being sent back to the end of the line because there were too many visitors. I understand that it's a famous museum that has many visitors going in and out but making it possible for that many people too book a ticket in a certain time slot that they cannot get in is something I can't understand.

Was okay experience. Extremely busy and awful getting to the Mona Lisa. They need to organize seeing her better. Just people pushing and not caring. We also couldn't see the whole museum. It's a lie when they say you can get through it in 2 hours. It's not possible. PLEASE MAKE THIS A BETTER EXPERIENCE FOR TOURISTS.

Great place to visit for anyone interested in arts. But to view the complete museum, set aside at least 6 hrs.
Reduced rating since almost all the staff at the museum were not approachable. The staff at the entrance are super friendly. However, inside the museum, only one out of six staff members that we approached for directions were willing to help. Pity that there are very limited (to no) options for food and drink when in the museum. Consider an additional 1-1,5 hours to move around the museum for people in wheelchairs or with baby strollers.

Very good museum, but twenty minute before exit time, the room with Mona Lisa close. Rude personal and ridicolous rule. Furthermore the organization of all museum is bad and the indication aren't understandable and confused. Unpleasant experience.

Be very careful booking advanced tickets.  I'm excited to visit but had an error when booking tickets, accidentally booked for Friday instead of Thursday.  I immediately noticed and sent a note to customer service asking if they could be changed or refunded so I could repurchase the correct date.  I was told that would not be possible.

DON’T go there. Today was a mess with so many people trying to get in.
When you book a block time (which we did) the doors are still open to thousands of others. This is not funny anymore for anyone.
The museum is making a fortune but you can’t see a thing. Don’t go there until they stick to block times only.

2nd time visiting. For some reason their audio guide is now on a Nintendo 3DS, the new guide is useless. The tutorial states it has functions which it doesn’t and there is probably about 5% of pieces with any information, if that. The map of the museum is useless too, stated there were places to get food and drink where there weren’t and then randomly areas would be closed with a security guard sat in front of a door saying it’s closed but not saying why.

Overhyped and overrated.

Honestly could've given this a miss. Way too busy and still queued for a while despite buying tickets in advance. Difficult to find our way around the museum - especially the exit! The building is beautiful and I enjoyed looking at it more than the art itself. There is too much art to truly appreciate any of it. And don't get me started on the Mona Lisa!
The collection is amazing.  You can get lost here.  Services are horrible though.  5 sinks with no soap.  Locked bathrooms.  No toilet paper.

Over estated 1:30 hours to get in the card system broke down and you should have cash only ...and you have to go out of the museum and wait another 1:30 hours ....etc .The employees security officer are rude and doesn't speak any other language except French .... well-done guys

Terrific items on display, putting aside the fact that they are stolen from the nations who created the civilization.

For a museum with so many international pieces on display, and so many international visitors, many display items lack non-French description.

Signs are not well- designed. Exiting the building was a bit confusing!

The staff were not polite and helpful specially in the enters. I was with a stroller with two kids and there were no specific queue or priority. I was asked to use the escalator which is not safe with the stroller instead of elevator.
Very crowded and expensive (22 eu). The self guiding device they rent ( another 9 eu) was basically worthless. Personal were not helpful at all.

Sprawling museum, mostly art. Unfortunately, the entrance was poorly organised. We bought tickets in advance but still had to wait outside in the scorching heat and sun. There were no signs outside or inside, the air conditioning was weak, and the staff was not very helpful.

The staff's behaviour was also disappointing.

This visit did not meet our expectations, and we won't be returning.

The historical monuments in it were absolutely beautiful. But the presentation of the works was of very poor quality. They just put the works in the rooms and dust covered everything. They didn't even bother to clean the dust on them. The lighting of the painting was also terrible. Some of the pictures were in the dark, not visible. People gathered in one place to see the Mona Lisa, but no one cares much about the rest of the historical monuments.

Ps: It's not about Louvre but in general Paris is such a dirty place. Everywhere is smelling so bad and their transportation system is so expensive. And for every beautiful thing you have to pay too much. I didn't like Paris at all.

My daughter is in a wheelchair and this museum cannot be considered accessible or barrier-free. Multiple lifts/elevators were out of service, and those that were working tended to be complicated and very confusing. There were even places where there weren't any lifts such as the Great Sphinx of Tanis. Sometimes, we even had to go up and down a few times to get to a certain area because of a broken elevator.

The exhibits were breathtaking and truly worth coming to France and the employees were mostly helpful, but the Louvre is not a wheelchair friendly place, although it is the year of 2024 Paris Olympics and Paralympics.

Still, we had a pretty good time, and aim to come back some day, and I hope that when we do, the Louvre will have become a more welcoming place for people with disabilities, especially for people in wheelchairs.

Why there is no cue line for the Mona Lisa and why do you wait in a greenhouse when you walk in? The world may never know.

Obviously fantastic display of art but the crowds and chaos cheapened a once in a lifetime visit.

Nice art and crafts museum.
DO NOT BUY TICKETS IN ADVANCE. You still wait 1 hr+

The queuing was quite awful, I don’t understand how they can’t employ the same type of entry like the Rijksmuseum. Secondly the attendees are just horribly rude, blocking stairs and doorways to get their pic for instagram.

Sorry Louvre but not good access for people with mobility issues, the elderly and the like. Stairs everywhere and more elevators out of order, than those working ! Not enough bathrooms (lines 10 deep), poor poor selection of food options in Paris of all places! :( The staff are so outnumbered and know next to nothing about the treasures they show.

Compare the Louvre to Madrid's Prado where you have easy access to all parts of the museum. No bathroom wait and great dining choices and certainly masterpieces galore! Add to that a wonderfully educated Prado staff sharing recommendations about other work to see, detail about the artists and so on. Prado you could teach the Louvre quite a bit about welcoming visitors !

I was so sorry to see this national jewel of France in such bad shape.

Amazing art but terrible museum. Once you are inside you cannot find a decent place to sit and have a snack, people sit on the floor to eat. The staff are mainly abrupt and impolite. Too crowded and signage is unclear, the audioguide is not user friendly. If you are claustrophobic, avoid this museum.

Is a good museum if everything you want is a couple of selfies. It's overcrowded, and everyone just tries to get a pic with a couple of famous paintings. The member of staff started shouting at me and ushered me out of the room where Mona Lisa is, even though I wasn't in the queue, wasn't taking pictures, and just tried to look at other paintings in the room. It's what museums are for, after all.
If you move out of the beaten path, you can find true gems, albeit less famous. For example, the second floor was practically empty, and way more comfortable. Overall, it feels like a place where people come to make a selfie with three artworks and make instagram pictures. Comparing to many European museums, this is less of a museum and more of a place to take photos, unfortunately.
However, as mentioned above, there are absolutely magnificent paintings outside of where most tourists go. Also, the building itself is worth seeing, both the exterior and the interior.
The attitude of staff really needs to be improved, though. I don't want to be shouted at for looking at painting in the art museum.

Tout d' abord je trouve que la sécurité a l ' entrée est un peu légere pour un musée 1 seul billet de vérifier sur 3 ?! Une fois a l' intérieur et malgré le plan très difficile de se repérer, quelques agents du louvre dans les salles au téléphone ( conversation perso) pendant leurs heures de travail ?! Cela reste une minorité mais je trouve que ce n est pas très professionnel, sinon le reste des agents très sympathique vous oriente avec le sourire, musée avec des plafonds magnifiques 🤩. A faire quand même une fois ....

I visited this museum recently, thinking it would be a good place to go. There was a vast collection of Egyptian antiquities, and free wheelchairs, which was useful as we had older members of my family, however this place is a nightmare to navigate with wheelchairs. The map isn’t clear enough for the massive halls and vast array of spaces, and there are no wheelchair disability friendly options. The lifts are not labelled enough and can be a bit small for wheelchairs. If travelling with wheelchairs, do not purchase the audio guide as it only has paths for more able bodied people. Being in a wheelchair makes everything take about 3 times as long for everyone else. Because of this, we had to take a taxi to the Eiffel Tower as we didn’t have time for the tour bus. I wouldn’t go back here. If you are planning to go I wouldn’t book anything after this for the day.
no chance to get there. not moving, huge queue. I've been to the British Museum and Hermitage, I know how queues look like, but this was hopeless.

I spent pretty much 4.5 hours power walking through this museum, mostly because I found relatively little that interested me. The museum is large (4.5 hours is needed to see it all, with limited stopping), and heavily uncurated. Unless you study particular periods and need to see things for that reason, or particularly like sculpture, the museum is underwhelming. That’s not to say it doesnt have some amazing historical artifacts! Its just laid out poorly, is exhausting to explore, features tons of dead ends and stairs that are poorly marked on the map, and generally has relatively few good paintings. In addition, a lot of the pieces in the museum are pretty much plundered from other civilizations, which adds a bit of a weird side to this being one of the pinnacles of French culture.

The audio guide is terrible to navigate and made the whole visit a dreadful experience. Instead of being able to focus on the exhibition, one is constantly preoccupied with figuring out what is being shown on the screen. Instead of being fancy with the Nintendo-based guide, a classical audio guide where you simply type in the number of the exhibition piece would have done a much better job.

Some of the exhibition itself is great. Being such a huge place though, it's easy to lose focus and become overwhelmed by the sheer amount of input.

Some tips:
Book tickets beforehand
Enter the musium from the exit (close to the metro)
IMPORTANT if you want to enjoy it go in the early hours of the day .
My opinion:
It is too crowded with people who only want to show of and take pictures.I went there to look at the ,, death of Marat" . The staff didn't know where curtain expositions are or didn't speak English ( an exception is a girl who was very kind and put together thoughtfully)

Biggest disappointment of my trip to Paris. it was really crowded and loud, so the atmosphere of museum simply wasn’t there. It was really hard to concentrate on art. Unfortunately, the staff working there wasn’t paying too much attention, so basically you could have entered without any ticket.

The art is great no question here, but there's a lot of people so we can't really enjoy the visit.
Plus, The most visited art is Mona Lisa and we couldn't see it for more than 2 minutes.

Advice, don't visit it in summer.

Got late by a couple minutes in the opening of the museum, got barred in entry, organizers said they would place my group in the priority queue to go on the next tour. Waited for 1h and still wasn't able to join in the tour. Left. 17€ wasted.
Best Queue Simulator Europe 2023.
My rating is just for the line as I’m not even inside yet. Even with prepaid “individual tickets”, it’s 2hrs to get in. Wish they would offer refunds in situations like this.
INFORMIERT EUCH VORHER WELCHE TEILE ÜBERHAUPT OFFEN HABEN. Wir wollten sehr gerne in die Ägyptische Abteilung. Diese war jedoch zu. Genauso wie mindestens ein viertel des Museums.
We were excited to visit the Louvre, but it was extremely crowded. The line was so long that we decided to admire the museum from outside only. The main attraction for me was the glass pyramid.

I entered through a gate other than the largest one, and I couldn't find any entrance sign at the gate so me and other people next to me were all embarrassed and took several minutes to find where entrance was. So I was 10 minutes late because of it, and the staff at the pyramid said us to go inside of the building so we said thanks and went toward the main building. But the (damn) staff at the main building denied us to enter and said us to go back to the pyramid. We went back but, in this time, the staff at the pyramid said the same as before.  The thing is that we were 10 minutes late but we were to skip standing in line at the pyramid and enter to the main building directly because we made reservation in advance and had ticket anyway, But the staff at the main building denied us to enter on his own term (maybe because we were late 10 minutes). Finally a female staff came to us and mitigated the conflict and took us to the main building guy and asked us "this man told you that you can't enter?" and I said yes and I told the main building staff that "You said 'go back to the pyramid!' "  Then he couldn't say anything nor say sorry. Thanks to the female staff I finally entered, but I saw he denied a few more tourists for the same reasons. Why does he do that? I can't understand. Louvere Museum itself was amazing and well-designed but the staff at the entrance ruined the image of the museum in my mind.

Most recent visit so so so crowded. Large groups off of buses. Shocking really how those groups walk , em mass, like a block-preventing others from seeing or moving freely. I’ve visited many times over the years. Beyond the tide foreign crowds & yes, I’m foreign-too, it was hot hot humid & no ventilation.
Of course, it’s the lou Re. The art is magnificent. My experience /not fun. Not engaging. Couldn’t wait to get outta there & into the open air.
You’re compelled to visit if you’re in Paris.
Although, my next trip the following spring -I skipped it. Didn’t miss a thing.

For the good parts, very good information for tourists. Clear lines and queues. The bad and ugly:  Maps they provide are quite useless. The layout is confusing. Most of the things, to me, are overrated. There was no way to get drinking water inside unless you paid €3.90 for 1 liter. No water fountains. The wheelchair lifts weren't working in places. Overall, quite poor.
stunning art pieces but too overhyped. Too crowded and not enjoyable. Queue is too long, there are better place in Paris.

Way too overcrowded and poorly ventilated. In addition, the maps that are given are terrible at best. They do not show all the rooms and it is easy to get lost in the museum. Fortunately the staff is friendly for the most part, not all of them however. The line up to see the Mona Lisa is ridiculous and when you finally get to see it, you have 10 seconds at best. The rest of the museum is practically empty in comparison so that's why I give it 2 stars. The Venus de Milo was nice.

Of course it's one of the top five museums in the world. And it's been completely ruined by tourists. Think of Grand Central Station at rush hour but with rudeness on top - I was looking at a painting with my group when I was literally shoved aside and CLIMBED OVER by someone who wanted to take a picture and then run away. And forget about the Mona Lisa. That scene is a disgrace. Of all the things there are to do and see in Paris, don't waste your time and money here.

I mean, seriously the Louvre with such a bad management of visitors circulation, paths, and indications? The app for the museum map wasn't working, including different phones trial. No printed flyers in any information point. The maps I found around the museum about the different floors and rooms, were NOT CLEAR at all. No clear signs on how to go through the museum, apart from little printed paper saying "Mona Lisa -->"... Like, seriously? The cherry on the cake was trying to exit the museum. 10 min walking underground ending about 3 streets away from where the museum was. No signs at all during that exit.

Come here to experience what an amazing collection of artifacts and paintings can feel like a nuicence. The place itself is very (very) large. It may take you days and still you may not cover the entire museum. Partially due to the sheer number of exhibits and mostly due to serious lack of any marking or direction indicators, or room numbering. The place is grossly mismanaged, not the preservation or maintain an e of the exhibits themselves, but the management of accessibility and layout. If you don't know / understand French you should buy the recorded guide tour. Be prepared to get lost a few times and waste your time.

This place needs more elevators due to how busy and popular this place is as well as a better finding to other elevators in this museum.  I could not find many of them there.  It was like a maze there.
Incredible museum with world-famous art and stunning architecture. It’s huge and can get very crowded, but it’s worth the visit. Just be prepared.. the toilets are a disaster

Oh my word, horrific queues, crowds, heat and noise. Sure, see the Mona Lisa and Venus De Milo but it is labythrine to navigate, packed and it is very badly curated.

Unbelievably, it is atrocious difficult to exit and I am proficient at French and know this part of Paris very well.

Prebook your ticket and go early to avoid the tours and crowds.

Leider nicht empfehlenswert.

Trotz Online-Ticket mussten wir an einem Wochentag über eine Stunde warten, bevor wir überhaupt das Gebäude betreten konnten. Die Organisation wirkte insgesamt wenig durchdacht.

Das Museum war zudem extrem überlaufen, insbesondere durch große Touristengruppen und Schulklassen. Viele Besucher verhielten sich entsprechend laut, sodass es kaum möglich war, die Ausstellungen in Ruhe zu betrachten und auf sich wirken zu lassen. Stattdessen wurde man ständig von anderen Besuchern gedrängt, die vor allem das perfekte Foto machen wollten.

Auch die Beschilderung ließ deutlich zu wünschen übrig. Trotz Lageplan war die Orientierung innerhalb des Gebäudes kompliziert, da viele Bereiche verwinkelt sind und die Wege nicht klar gekennzeichnet werden.

Besonders enttäuschend war der Bereich rund um die Mona Lisa. Die Wartezeit war sehr lang, und der Raum selbst war völlig überfüllt. Ein entspanntes Betrachten des Kunstwerks war praktisch unmöglich.

Hinzu kommt, dass das Personal teilweise unfreundlich wirkte und die Besucher eher zügig durch die Räume leitete, anstatt für ein angenehmes Besuchserlebnis zu sorgen.

Auch die Gestaltung einiger Ausstellungsbereiche erschien stellenweise lieblos und wenig durchdacht. Ein stimmiges Gesamtkonzept war nicht immer erkennbar.

Insgesamt steht das Preis-Leistungs-Verhältnis in keinem angemessenen Verhältnis zum gebotenen Erlebnis. Für uns war der Besuch leider eine große Enttäuschung.

Incredible and enormous musea, but i give it only 2 stars, why?

It was outside 20 degrees but inside it was extremly hot, because there was no ventilation and overcrowded with people. It felt at some point i was in Death Valley with the temperatures inside

The musea felt like a maze, no directions and half of the objects are only in French, so i had to translate it to English with my phone

Also the WC's was incredible dirty everywhere. It was a big mess, didnt expect that for the most famous musea in the world.

And the treasure room was still closed and for no reason access to the Egypt rooms was also closed today (on a monday, the guard said closed and nothing more)

That was a big bummer because i was excited to see the Egypt history

I expected more of the Louvre but it was a big let down. Never meet your heroes they say.

One of the most famous and important museums of the world, entrance is 22 euros, it was very crowded and with some areas closed. Most of the elevators were not working and with a pregnant person it’s stressful and tiring. They should fix it.

I was so excited to see El Greco's paintings! I was thrilled with emotion to find the room where his 3 works of art can be seen at Louvre Museum. Only that this time they could not be seen. The acces was blocked, with no prior notice anywhere. The reason was unbelievable - "we are short on staff". I call this very poor management and disconsidering your visitors. Of course one can understand when certains areas in such a big museum are closed for maintenance or whatever serious reasons - but to just close the acces to El Greco's paintings because you are short on staff...wow!...It happened in 30 may 2026. As in the pictures.

What’s the point of buying tickets in advance if you have to wait for more than one hour outside in windy and rainy weather?

We arrived 30 mins in advance but were let in 40 mins after our designated time slot. Very poor organization overall - should either hire more securities to fasten the entrance or sell less tickets. At the end of the day we were left with very little time to explore the exhibitions, especially taking into consideration that some halls close 30 mins before the declared time, so you can only guess if you’ll be able to visit all the rooms you planned to visit.

The museum is fascinating though!

the menagment of the museum is terrible. they kicked us out from some rooms without giving any explanation, closing stairs, lift and doors. you cant understand nothing on the map, is so confusing. there is a lot of art, but is impossible to visit everything, you get halfway without having seen many things and still exhausted.
if you want to go, i recommend a guide (not an audio guide) and to go there in the morning. also bring your own food, the bars inside are expensive.

art is always beautiful

Well, my rate of one star will be exclusively for the Louvre management. These guys deserve nothing but being fired. We had tickets booked for June 16, That day when strike or whatever happened and the doors were locked until 3pm. I am not against worker strikes, people can and must fight for their rights what did management of the museum and that day? Right, nothing. They didn’t communicate to thousands of people waiting all day in line they didn’t tell anything to security and stuff whats going on. Management was waiting another strike happens but now outside with visitors. And it almost happened. People in line was very nervous: no information if museum will open, no info what was going on, no info if you have to request refund, reschedule etc. That day Louvre showed stratospheric level incompetence. Very disappointing. The museum itself is great and still worth to visit but people who manage it they make this place terrible.

Poorly laid out, rude rude rude staff, this is a terrible experience for disabled people. You have to hand in your passport to use any disabled aids. Staff refused us access to disabled lifts without proof of disability!!! I wouldn't recommend at all. Uk museums, are much better designed and free.

I never have such a horrible experience as with this museum. People who work there care less about people in general.
We have our tickets bought online many months prior. So 16 of June Monday we were here 8-30 to enter 9-00. It was very hot around 30 C and sunny day. So 10-00 museum is not open. 11-00 museum is not open and people grow more impatient. People outside who were working for museum have no clue why museum is not open and when it will be opening if ever on that day.
We were waiting till 12-00 (3,5 hours outside on the sun on our legs) without any information from museum workers.
So we left in frustration because that day we suppose to leave on the train later on and only bc we wanted to see this museum we decided to spend whole day in museum without hotel otherwise we would leave early morning.
So it was horrible experience and day were ruined !
Later on 17-00 we saw the article that museum never opened that day and workers just conduct a strike without saying thousands of people waiting outside that it has no sense to wait.
So when we wrote about refund for our tickets we got letter saying they will think about it and it will take months!!!
So I will never come back in this museum where people who craving for art treated in this disrespectful dismissive way! I visited many great museums all over the world and it is unacceptable how this museum treats his costumers and people in general!

I bought a ticket in advance to join the 'fast' line at a set time. I arrived 30 mins early, sat in the shade abd watched the fast and slow lines move at the same speed - slow. The lines are unsweetened from the sun. I am a senior. I would not be able to stand for over 30 minutes in the sunshine, in that fast line. So I forfeited my ticket and didnt go in, disappointed by the way lines were poorly sheltered and handled

Absolutely horrible experience. I tried to buy a ticket for a future date because I heard it is hard to get one close to your visit. Their website is very confusing. I accidentally purchased a ticket for the wrong date. They were unsupportive and stated- no refunds under any circumstances, and mistakes don’t matter. What kind of company operates like that. If you buy a ticket and do not use it you should be able to get a refund. What if a family member had died and I could not go on the ticket date- they would still say we don’t care and screw you - incredible they can operate like this! I visited Paris but didn’t realize my mistake until the day before I was set to visit the museum- and of course there were no tickets available when I realized my unused ticket date had passed. Extremely disappointed at going to Paris with the primary purpose of visiting the Louvre and being treated so horribly with no visit. I did get to see the Arch du Triumph though- it was really cool!

Great art

TERRIBLE SECURITY...

and VERY French attitudes.

If you're wondering why the museum was robbed, here you have it: security guards doing nothing that vaguely looks like security.

(I watched them for 10 minutes each and neither looked up once.)

Mention something, and they're incensed.

Wer westliche Geschichte und christliche Kunst schätzt, wird hier voll auf seine Kosten kommen. Für uns war die Sammlung etwas zu einseitig und eintönig. Beeindruckend in seiner Grösse, aber nicht für jeden Geschmack.
Organisatorisch gibt es Luft nach oben: Der Audioguide war online nicht buchbar, und herauszufinden wo man ihn abholen kann, hat unnötig viel Zeit und Energie gekostet. Auch die WC-Anlagen könnten sauberer sein.

I am sorry that the family experience was ruined by inept workers who are either underpaid and therefore completely lack enthusiasm, or simply do not like their job. It all started one day with a young woman at the entrance of the Louvre, white with brown hair, who was completely unhelpful and told us that tickets could only be purchased via an online QR code. She never mentioned anything about buying them in person, even though I asked her multiple times; her responses were short and she had a bad attitude.
The next day, we had already bought them online, but the tickets wouldn't arrive—only the purchase confirmation. In person, they informed us that it was a third-party purchase and that they couldn't do anything about it. We had to wait in line and buy the tickets all over again.
But just when you think the mistreatment in such a famous museum is over, a person—if you can even call them that—with a beard, started getting angry because my underage siblings were waiting in the shade while my parents stood in line. He started shouting, saying we couldn't enter. He spoke to the supervisor, whom we had already talked to previously, and the supervisor indicated that we could indeed pass.
It's a shame that the Mona Lisa, such a great work of art, is in a museum that has not only been robbed multiple times due to the ineptitude of its workers, but also mistreats tourists. I would be ashamed if someone visiting my country was treated that way. I hope they can change the staff or raise their salaries so they are in a better mood.

This place made me hate myself. Why did I come here? To see the Mona Lisa? Yeah right, good luck with the crowds. To see the Napoleon III Apartments? Nope, they’re closed today and you will never find staff to tell you that as you wander around for hours trying to find it. We love history and going to museums and I left learning nothing! Also, when you finally think you’ve found the exit you’ll find yourself in a mall that you can’t easily escape either.

I want to express my utmost disappointment regarding our visit to the Louvre on May 8th. While being in New York my husband and I booked museum tickets in advance. Also, we new that every first Friday of the month, entry to museum is free. On April 10 at 11:30 PM when  we opened Louvre official website it was stated that, due to the fact that the first Friday of May is a public holiday (May 1st) the free entry for all categories is moved from May 1st to May 8th from 6 PM to 9 PM. We booked the free tickets for 6 PM, printed them out, and received confirmation and receipt by email. We didn’t have any issues getting into the museum, but at the final ticket checkpoint to the Denon wing we were stopped and told that our tickets are not valid. I showed him the confirmation and receipt but he responded in a nervous and even rude manner, saying that today there is no free entry and we must go to the booking office. When I asked him what his name was, he flipped his ID around. In the booking office, we spoke with chef d'quipe named Benjamin Favreau. He was very polite but still didn’t help us. He even called IT department and IT also could not explain why the tickets were issued. Finally after losing 50 minutes we bought regular tickets and entered museum for remaining two hours. The man at the checkpoint near the Denon wing again turned his ID to the other side as we approached. I hope the Louvre's management will look into this situation.

It is extremely crowded. They’re letting too many people in at once, and it completely ruins the experience. You can barely move. While waiting to see the Mona Lisa, the staff were very rude and were literally yelling at people to hurry up. I paid for this experience and would actually like to enjoy. Organize the viewing area and crowd flow better.

We came to France to visit the Louvre Museum, but unfortunately, my son injured his leg on the way and was unable to walk. During our visit, we were able to borrow a wheelchair, which was very helpful. However, when we returned the wheelchair upon exiting, we discovered that the elevator was out of order. We had no choice but to use the escalator, and due to the heavy crowd, my son was pushed and fell, getting hurt again.

Inside the museum, especially near the Mona Lisa, the crowd was so overwhelming that the wheelchair was constantly pushed around, making it nearly impossible to properly see the painting.

Though the Louvre is one of the world’s most famous museums, for a family with mobility challenges, it sadly became one of our most difficult and heartbreaking experiences. We hope the museum can improve accessibility and crowd control for visitors with special needs

We went with a baby stroller, and the accessibility is the standard for this kind of place (lifts on entrance and for the main rooms). The place is crowded, noisy, main rooms are hot (not good if you are planning to take children to visit the place) and the staff is rude and not that helpful. Taking in consideration other museums we visited, this one is really disappointing and does not live up to its name.

I had a poor experience booking tickets through this website.
During checkout, the system changed the date of my ticket without clear notice. I was not given a clear confirmation step to verify the correct date before payment. As a result, I ended up with a ticket for the wrong day.
The site is not user friendly. You are required to create an account, yet there is no simple way to confirm your booking details before completing the purchase. This creates unnecessary risk for errors.
I contacted customer service to explain the issue. They refused to offer a refund or correction, even though the problem came from their system and not from my actions.
As a customer, I did not receive the product I intended to purchase. The process lacks basic safeguards and the support response did not address the issue.
I would not recommend booking through their site because they are not responsible for any issues you may have. I will also add that I am a student and do not have alot of money, so I feel scammed.

A visit to the Louvre on 16 February – or rather, an attempt at one: my daughter and I made a special trip to Paris. We arrived with pre-booked tickets, only to find huge crowds and a short notice strike. I was given a QR code and told to get a refund for the ticket. To do something like this at such short notice is simply outrageous.

Then, at least we tried to get the ticket refunded, we got the information by mail that we would not get a refund because they opened about one hour later again for the public.
Even my point that the staff had instructed us and handed us a QR code fell on deaf ears. That was simply the Louvre’s policy. Of course, the Louvre’s policies have nothing to do with the law and of course, it is not correct.

We wrote to them three times, more out of principle than for the money, and received the same ignorant, arrogant replies. The Louvre really ought to be ashamed of itself when it comes to customer service, and of putting its staff in a position where they have to treat customers in this way. It’s a disgrace for such a hospitable city with such excellent cuisine that a flagship museum doesn’t even know the basics of hospitality.

Greetings, the first big disappointment is that it was not possible to see the entire Egyptian exhibition, because it was closed. The second disappointment is the disorganized viewing of the painting by Leonardo da Vinci - Mona Lisa. You have to wait a long time in a crowd without any order, everyone is pushing each other and there is not enough space to take pictures in front of the painting itself. This could have been solved much better because for many the most important thing to see in the Louvre Museum is the Mona Lisa.

Nice layout, Beautiful painting like the Mona Lisa. Experience ruined by one blond employee being ridiculously strict about tickets while we were trying to enter with our valid museum tickets. We were with a guided tour so it made the experience even worse. This employee felt like she had an agenda against my tour guide. Single bad employee in a sea of good ones. Had to encounter her. This was the last straw on the camel’s back making me never recommending Paris because of this and many bad experiences.

By the way, did it with a different employee and went in.

The place is overcrowded making it even more exhausting. This place is not your average stroll but a war zone to see important art pieces.

Be prepared if you want to go. Do not buy water here, the lines for the cafes are ridiculously long. Remember to bring your water.

Anyways one star, Museum can do better by moving the some important pieces somewhere else. So the museum is less crowded.

By the way date of visit: 29th of December 2024

I was really excited to go to "One of the best museums in the world" and it turned out to be the most dissapointed experience.First of all the maps are ridiciously unhelpful with side perspectives of the museum and very few rooms have numbers and labels and if so tiny labels on top.Second as a tourist coming from Greece I was so dissapointed to realise that the majority if not all the descriptions are in french so I mostly admired the art instead of learing its history.Thind the toilets are so dirty and impractical for a massive museum.And lastly so many important pieces of art are just there with no unique room or presentation making them just another thing for you to see.Even The mona lisa is just in the wall with all the other pieces.Overall the palace looks gorgeous.The quantity of the artifacts is very impressive but especially for foreingers the museum is not friendly at all.
