The staff by La Gioconda were so mean!!!!! To was waiting for my daughter in line to take her photos. He heard it and just pulled her out of the line that she has been waiting for such a long time. I told him that he should’t do it. He said I asked him to do. I understand him that he wanted to let us leave asap. However, his behavior as so rude and ruined our day of the tour.

Es werden zu viele Karten für die selbe Zeit verkauft! Die Massen der Besucher machen es nicht möglich das Museum zu erleben und genießen. Ich war in Luvre zum ersten Mal vor zwanzig Jahren und damals war es eine andere Atmosphäre! Heute werden Salfies gemacht, es ist laut, viele Eltern mit kleinen Kindern !? Es ist schade, aber Lovre zu besuchen kann man sich sparen.

El museo del Louvre es muy bonito. Es muy bonito, muy bonito, bonito. También es muy bonito y es bonito, bonito, bonito. Bonito del norte y bonito del sur. Extremadamente bonito y muy estupendamente estúpido. Saben ustedes que,en realidad, lo que tienen allá es un enorme parque de atracciones, tipo Disneyland, lleno de turistas que se atropellan unos a otros por un condenado selfie frente a la Mona Lisa? Bueno, es un decir. Un selfie frente a una multitud de turistas que a su vez intentan sacarse miles de selfies con o junto a la Mona Lisa, o se hacen videos para las redes sociales, ignorado todo lo expuesto en esta sala y en el resto de las salas. Esto es la corrupción y degradación del arte servido a las masas. Señores, entiendo que ustedes tienen que hacer caja porque un museo no se mantiene sólo a base de subvenciones ni vive del aire, pero por comparación, nunca vi tal aluvión de gente maleducada e irrespetuosa con el arte ni en el Museo del Prado, ni en el British Museum! Este museo es una alegoría a la condición humana, donde lo expuesto no son las obras de arte, sino la de sandez implícita de quienes lo visitan, de forma masificada, sólo por crear contenidos en sus miserables redes sociales... Por lo tanto, todo es bonito. Muy bonito, bonito. Es bonito. Pero de verdad. Bonito es. Es bonito. Bonito es. Para no volver.

Für uns eine Enttäuschung, wir konnten nicht rein, es war unmöglich so lange in einer riesigen Schlange bei 30 Grad in praller Sonne warten zu müssen. Das muss anders organisiert werden oder für Sonnenschutz oder Kühlung gesorgt werden. Ansonsten kippen die Menschen um.

L’ho trovato molto confusionario nella gestione dei percorsi per i visitatori; inoltre incommentabile il fatto che per poter “vedere” la Gioconda si debba lottare contro un esercito di visitatori pazzi tutti armati di smartphone per fotografare l’opera.
Create due file: una per aspiranti fotografi e l’altra per chi desidera semplicemente osservare veramente il dipinto!

One of the top attractions in Paris
Really and truly beautiful architecture
Such an iconic museum. Can easily spend hours here. If you’re only in Paris for a short time, try to plan ahead on what you want to see and follow that path.

Of course when visiting Paris, one must stop by the Louvre!

There is no review/words that would ever do this museum justice. It's simply breathtaking, just the building itself before you ever enter any of the exhibit halls.

If you are someone who loves taking pictures (where you consider yourself a photographer or just obsessed with snapping footage on your cell phone) your phone will absolutely HATE to see the Louvre coming! Bring a portable charger because you WILL wear your battery OUT!!!

We specifically visited only for 2 things.

1st was the couture exhibit. This exhibit had on display designs from some of the TOP designers across numerous collections. If you love fashion, you are going to GAGGGGG over this exhibit! Unfortunately it does leave soon, not sure exactly when, so runnnnnnn!

2nd was the Mona Lisa herself of course. You don't fly all the way to Paris, hike a train to the Louvre, wait in line and do all this rigamaroe to not see "the smiling lady" herself! I, myself, was unimpressed personally with this particular piece, but I'm still glad I went to experience it. We literally went into the exhibit hall and STRAIGHT to the area it's kept at to look/take pictures.

You need MULTIPLE visits, lasting MULTIPLE hours, to fully experience the entire museum as it's soooooooo massive. It took us about 2 hours or so and all we saw was the couture exhibit (which we semi-kinda breezed through and didn't really stop to "take it all in") and the Mona Lisa.

If you're ever in Paris, I highly suggest you put this as a stop on your trip!

A great museum to visit if not for the Mona Lisa for the Napoleon era of French art and early Christian works.

The Persian and Egyptian works were a great surprise.

It's simply one of the greatest collections of artifacts ever brought together. Every item has a story. It would take days to go through it all, so I'd sincerely suggest a guide to maximize your time viewing the impressive collection. The most impressive
museum I’ve ever visited. With multiple wings showcasing a vast collection of art, sculptures, and paintings, it feels both overwhelming and inspiring. The scale is so extensive that one day simply isn’t enough to take it all in. It’s a must-see destination, and I’ll definitely be returning to explore more.

We went on a Friday night around 5:30. The line up wasn’t that long to get in, plenty of lockers available and room to mow around the museum.
Free locker storage was a great thing.
Once you’re in there are three wings to go to so you have to make some decisions.
We used Rick Steves audio tour as our guide. He has key items with great explanations. I could see how the musee’s audio guide would be great if you had all day and wanted to look at most of the art. We had only a few hours and a secretive guide was helpful!
Such incredible art. Must see.

We visited with a tour company City Wonders and an amazing guide, Anais who shared with us the wonders and stories of the museum. It was informative and very enjoyable.
The museum would take 4 days to properly see everything it offers. Incredible paintings and art. If you only have a day I recommend a tour group early in the day to get ahead of the crowds a bit. But it is a must visit for anyone who comes to Paris.

Fabulous complex with impressive grounds. One visit is most definitely not enough as there is so much to see! There is so much detail to the building itself. Benches are placed throughout the museum to rest. I definitely recommend exploring and then taking a break, having something to eat from the cafe, and then continuing.

I would highly recommend getting a 2-3 hour guided tour from Viator. We had the best guide who was so knowledgeable about the museum and gave us the history of the museum when it was a palace. She also made sure to show us all the important details in some of the famous paintings. Best 3 hours spent!
Loved it! Must visit while in Paris! But please wear comfortable shoes because you will walk a lot ! And get a fan when visiting in summer

Very beautiful art place.
Better buy ticket first because the line was super long
I waited almost 30 minutes to got in when I bought ticket on the spot.

Visited the Louvre Museum and overall it was an amazing experience. The museum is huge with incredible artworks and definitely worth visiting at least once. I got my tickets at the entrance and didn’t have to wait too long to get in. Going in the morning is the best time to avoid crowds.
Also, ticket prices can vary depending on whether you are an EU citizen or not.
Overall, a must-see landmark in Paris with a great experience if you plan your visit properly!

The museum is huge but worth all the time and effort!
You surely need around 4-5 hours at least to see everything and necessarily comfortable shoes!

A must-visit in Paris. I highly recommend getting the Paris Museum Pass and booking your entry time in advance—aim for the earliest slot (around 9 AM) to avoid crowds.

The Lion’s Gate (Porte des Lions) entrance was extremely efficient—I got through security and entered in under 5 minutes. Once inside, go upstairs directly to Room 711 to see the Mona Lisa early before it gets crowded.

Be aware that some galleries may be closed without prior notice on the official website. You’ll need to scan QR codes inside the museum to check closures, which can be confusing and may affect your planned route.

The museum is very large and quite complex to navigate. Even with a map, it’s easy to get lost, and some sections are not directly connected. You may need to take long detours, go up and down floors, and some elevators may be unavailable.

Even a quick visit can take 5–6 hours. If you want to fully appreciate the museum, one visit is definitely not enough.

***** PRINT YOUR PASS! *****

Nobody wants to see you trying to get a signal while you're in the line and worse yet when you're showing them screen shots from booking or viator which are not the passes to get in! See the pic, this is the only pass that will let you into the Louvre Museum.

Please be respectful and learn some basic French as the world comes to visit this location and the people trying to help you are just overwhelmed.

Culture, beauty, sensory overload! We were only here for 3 days, coming back as this was only a taste of what this magnificent country has to offer.

The Louvre Museum is one of those places that feels worth seeing just to experience it in person.

The building itself is impressive, and walking through the courtyard with the glass pyramid is a nice moment, even before going inside. It can be busy. We had planned to come to the Louvre free evening (first Friday of every month) but arriving we found the queue to already be ~1 to 2 hours long. So we opted to walk around and enjoy the sunset around the Louvre instead!

Even a short visit feels like you have seen something special while in Paris. However, if you are specifically wanting to go inside or plan to attend an event with high public interest. Make sure you have your tickets and arrive well in advance

Visiting the Louvre was truly an unforgettable experience. From the moment we saw the iconic glass pyramid, we were in awe. The architecture alone is stunning such a beautiful mix of historic and modern design.

Inside, every room felt like stepping into a different chapter of history. Seeing world-famous masterpieces like the Mona Lisa in person was surreal, but honestly, every gallery had something incredible to offer. The paintings, sculptures, and artifacts are so detailed and powerful you could spend hours just taking it all in.

The atmosphere is inspiring and magical. Even with so much to see, everything felt organized and easy to explore. Walking through the grand halls made us feel like we were part of something timeless.

If you’re ever in Paris, the Louvre is an absolute must-visit. It’s not just a museum it’s an experience you’ll never forget. 💛✨

Visiting the Louvre was an unforgettable experience. The museum is enormous and full of incredible masterpieces from different periods of history. Seeing famous works like the Mona Lisa and the Venus de Milo in person was truly special.

The architecture of the building itself is stunning, especially the glass pyramid at the entrance, which creates a beautiful contrast between modern and historic design. Even walking through the halls feels like traveling through centuries of art and culture.

It can get quite busy, so it’s best to go early in the morning and buy online your tickets as well. The organization inside is good and there are many signs to guide visitors. Inside is really warm so make sure you have light clothes. Whether you love art or just want to experience one of the most famous museums in the world, the Louvre is absolutely worth visiting.

Our visit to the Louvre Museum was such a memorable experience. The first time we came to Paris, we skipped it because we didn’t want to wait in the lines - but this time, we booked a timed entry, and it made all the difference. Even though the line looked long when we arrived, it moved very quickly.

If you’re booking online, I recommend going directly through the Louvre’s official website. Also, a helpful tip: visitors under 18 get in free.

Once inside, there’s a café with very delicious ham and cheese sandwiches - perfect for a quick snack before exploring the museum.

Of course, seeing the Mona Lisa is a must. You can get close, but it’s crowded. A great trick is to step slightly to the right side of the room - you’ll still get a close view without being right in the middle of the crowd.

Wear comfortable shoes, take advantage of the benches throughout the museum, and don’t rush. The Louvre is vast and meant to be experienced slowly - take your time and enjoy it ✨

Must go to place at least once in a lifetime, don’t get me wrong, it’s way too busy and it takes wayyy to long to get into considering you buy tickets for a timed entry, it’s not perfect. But what you get to see makes it all worth it and is an amazing thing you must do at least once in your lifetime.
The ammount of art and history in this place is beyond amazing. La Monalisa (if you want to see it) make it your first stop and be prepared to fight your way to the front. Other than that, enjoy.

Highly recommend getting tickets in advance. Prices are different for EU and non-EU members.

We've been here and didn't have to wait much, 10 min only, got tickets online for a specific time frame.

Highly recommend going in the morning - first, fewer people and fewer kids around, better to see everything without rush.

Avoid the main entrance, we came in from the metro station entrance and it was empty.

Recommend spending at least 2 hours to be able to see

Visited as a tourist with a group. Beautiful pieces of art are displayed here but it is a very large place! It is best to dedicate a whole day to this museum. With a tour group, I was not able to see many of the things that caught my attention.

The Louvre is an incredible museum with stunning art and history. Seeing famous masterpieces like the Mona Lisa was unforgettable. The building itself is beautiful, and the atmosphere is inspiring. A must-visit place in Paris for anyone who loves culture and art.

Book a tour with a Art historian for the evening to avoid the massive crowds. See the pictures posted, was only able to get a picture of the Mona Lisa because it was evening. Recommend booking with MEMQRIES, guide explained the differences between the different pieces of art plus information about the Louvre itse

Of course, great museum but really big. The best is to visit several times and concentrate on oneperiod or wing of the museum. If you go early, start from 3rd floor and you will be alone for at least half an hour. Skip Mona Lisa! Other Da Vinci paintings are more beautiful. And look for 'hidden' Renoir, Monet, Degas paintings ...

The Louvre is a breathtaking masterpiece of art and history. From the iconic glass Pyramid to the enigmatic Mona Lisa, every corner tells a story. While the crowds can be overwhelming, the vast collection of world-class treasures makes it an essential, unforgettable experience in Paris.

Oddly, you could spend a week in the expansive halls of The Louvre. I didn't realize that there are over 9 miles of space, so a highlights tour is recommended.
Mona Lisa seemed to be the focal artwork, Venus de Milo, plus a multitude of other paintings, sculptures, tapestries, and artifacts from all over the world.

We came without a ticket or reservation and walked right into the museum without having to wait. There's different wings with artwork from different times and periods. You can easily spend more than one afternoon there. It is free for EU citizens under age 26. For less waiting time take the alternate entrances.

Great Museum, you can stay there for all day long and you won't probably finish to visit it!
Not only Mona Lisa, but also other masterpieces are here, especially from Ancient Greece and Italy.
Approved!

The Louvre Museum is truly one of the most extraordinary places I’ve ever visited. From the moment you see the glass pyramid, you feel the weight of art, history, and culture all in one place. The collection is massive — from the Mona Lisa and Venus de Milo to countless hidden gems that most people walk past too quickly. The building itself is stunning, with grand halls and intricate architecture that make you feel like you’re walking through time. It’s easy to get lost, but that’s part of the magic. If you love art, culture, or simply beauty, the Louvre is an experience you’ll never forget.

Fifth time in Paris and I finally got to the Louvre! I was braced for crowds and long waits, but it was all very civilised. I was pleasantly surprised by how roomy and airy it is, with most rooms flooded with natural light. The "must see" items were well signposted and surprisingly accessible - I even managed a photo of the Mona Lisa without much of a wait! I liked that there was frequent seating, and quite a lot of toilets which was a nice change in Paris. I had a lovely time and would happily return to see some of the many exhibits I missed first time around.

The Louvre is everything an art lover dreams of and more. From the moment you see the glass pyramid entrance, you feel the weight of history and creativity. Inside, the collection is breathtaking—masterpieces like the Mona Lisa, the Winged Victory of Samothrace, and countless sculptures and paintings from every corner of the world.

The museum is vast yet thoughtfully organized, with helpful signage and friendly staff to guide you. I especially appreciated the audio guide and the calm courtyard spaces to pause between galleries. Whether you have a few hours or a full day, every visit reveals something new.

A must-see in Paris for anyone who appreciates art, architecture, or simply wants to stand in awe of human achievement.
Words can’t describe the experience in the most visited  museum in the world.  Went with a guide so entering was a breeze.  Saw only a fraction of things but left extremity happy.

Obviously, it’s full of amazing art and you should definitely visit but….
1. You wait in multiple lines. I appreciate that it is the most popular museum in the world, so there will be lines, but some are unnecessary, specifically, the lines to get into each wing. You’ve already queued up to get into the building and you know that you’ll need to queue again to see the Mona Lisa up close, for food, for audio, for the toilets. Why the dumb queue for each wing? So, if you have any physical issues that are exacerbated by extensive standing, just bear these additional queues in mind and plan accordingly. We had tickets for 10am and waited in the pyramid line for maybe 20-30 mins. But total waiting time for everything was about a hour (and we avoided some of the most popular exhibits). A fifth of our time in the museum was queueing. Choose what you really want to see, and see these first. Know where you need to be and plan the best way to get there.
2. There is zero visitor flow for either the Denon or Sully wings. Be prepared for a lot of doubling back. The Louvre loves dead end. I followed the exits signs in one part of the Denon for about 5 mins only to find myself facing a black painted partition that cut off access to “the exit” with no other information. It was weird and frustrating. I’ve never experienced it in any other museum. According to the broadsheet (museum guide) you are supplied with, it looks there is a visitor flow, but in reality, I found it otherwise.
3. This is my bad, but check your tickets and, I don’t know, google or something. Visiting the Louvre Museum in Paris is not just a trip to a museum — it’s a journey through thousands of years of human history, art, and civilization. I spent nearly 6 hours here and still felt like I had only scratched the surface. Whether you're an art enthusiast, a history buff, or simply curious about one of the most iconic museums in the world, the Louvre is an unforgettable experience.
✨ Highlights:
Of course, the Mona Lisa is a must-see. It's smaller than expected, but the crowd and the energy around it speak volumes about its importance. Go early if you want a decent view without too many heads in the way!
The Winged Victory of Samothrace and Venus de Milo are breathtaking in person. The scale, detail, and emotion carved into these ancient masterpieces are simply beyond words.
The Napoleon III Apartments were a total surprise — extravagant, ornate, and transporting. If you ever wondered how royalty lived, this section of the Louvre is your answer.
The Egyptian Antiquities collection is massive and beautifully curated. Mummies, sarcophagi, statues, and ancient artifacts are laid out in a way that tells a coherent story of the time.
🏛 Architecture:
The building itself is as impressive as the art it houses. The Louvre Palace is grand, elegant, and immense. Walking through its vast corridors, marble staircases, and vaulted ceilings feels like being in a movie. Don’t forget to look up — many ceilings are painted masterpieces in their own right.
🖼 Layout & Navigation:
The museum is HUGE. It’s easy to get lost, but honestly, that’s part of the fun. Grab a map or download the Louvre app ahead of time. The museum is divided into three wings — Denon, Sully, and Richelieu — each with its own treasures. Planning your route ahead of time can help you make the most of your visit.
⏱ Tips for Visiting:
Arrive early. Lines can be long, especially during peak tourist seasons. Booking tickets online in advance is highly recommended.
Wear comfortable shoes. You’ll be walking a lot.
Don’t rush. Prioritize what you want to see, and be okay with not seeing everything in one visit.
Bring water and snacks. The café options inside are pricey and get crowded fast.
🎟 Ticketing & Entry:
Security was efficient, and the entrance through the glass pyramid is iconic and smooth. Once inside, everything is well-signposted. Staff were friendly and helpful, though it’s a busy place, so be patient.
🌍 Overall Impression:
There are museums, and then there is the Louvre — a living archive of human creativity and history. No matter how much you prepare, nothing compares to standing in front of some of the most famous artworks in the world and feeling their power in person.
I would return again in a heartbeat, maybe focusing on just one wing per visit next time. This place is truly a once-in-a-lifetime experience — or maybe a dozen times in a lifetime if you’re lucky.

The Louvre is undoubtedly a world-famous museum and a must-visit landmark in Paris. That said, it is extremely crowded almost all the time, which can make the overall experience quite tiring. Personally, I preferred Musée d’Orsay for its diversity of exhibits and themes.

While most visitors naturally focus on the artworks displayed on the walls, I would also recommend paying attention to the ceilings. Some of the most impressive details in the Louvre are actually above you, and they are easy to miss if you only follow the crowds from one famous artwork to another.

For those who want to take good photos of the Louvre exterior, early morning is probably the best time. Also, please note that the surrounding area may not be accessible at night due to recent security arrangements, so do not rely on late-night access if you are planning to take night photos of the Louvre.

Overall, the Louvre is still worth visiting for its historical importance and scale, but be prepared for heavy crowds and plan your timing carefully.

It seems ludicrous to write a review about the Louvre, but I've done dumber things. Like writing a review about Nothing, Arizona, when there was literally nothing. The opposite is true here as the Louvre is everything an art museum should be. With over 35,000 pieces and over nine miles of exhibition space, it can be overwhelming, but some pre-planning helped make the most of the three-plus hours my husband and I spent here.

First things first, buy time-stamped tickets as soon as you know when you'll be in Paris. That's what we did, and it allowed us to go at the earliest time possible to avoid the worst of the crowds. Check to see if the Porte des Lions is open. Far less used than the glass pyramid, that's the side entrance closest to the Denon Wing where most of the famous works are. Currently Google Maps states it's temporarily closed, but it's worth checking.

Well before coming here, you should decide what the essential works are you want to see. Meandering the Louvre without a plan will tucker you out, and seating is at a premium. My husband and I wanted to see the acknowledged Top 10 works on a navigational path that allowed us time to browse with minimal backtracking. We started on Level 1 of the Denon Wing and sprinted to Room 711. That's where Leonardo da Vinci's "Mona Lisa" hangs.

It's small, under glass, and you can see it only from a restricted distance. But believe me, the crowd you see first thing in the morning reflects the best time to check this masterpiece off your list. Flip around and enjoy the museum's largest painting, "The Wedding Feast in Cana" by Veronese. Head to the back of the room and make a right to approach "The Winged Victory of Samothrace" in Room 703, a truly majestic sight.

This was where Audrey Hepburn floated down the steps in her red gown and gossamer shawl in "Funny Face". Walk beyond the mythic statue and make a right into the glorious Room 705 where you get up close to the resplendent French Crown Jewels. Walk back out and head to Room 702 where you see "The Coronation of Napoleon", a massive historical recreation by Jacques-Louis David. Continue to Room 700 and see two masterpieces.

The uplifting "Liberty Leading the People" by Eugene Delacroix and the darkly tragic "The Raft of the Medusa" by Theodore Gericault. Go downstairs to Room 403 on Level 0 still in the Denon Wing. That's where we saw the beautiful neoclassical sculpture, "Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss" by Antonio Canova. Walk over to Room 345 and join the admiring crowd ogling the Venus de Milo.

At this point, we allowed ourselves to roam among the Roman, Greek, and Egyptian antiquities. It's an expansive space and hard to maneuver if you're looking for pieces in particular. We found the Great Sphinx of Tanis in Room 338 of the Sully Wing and eventually the Code of Hammurabi from ancient Mesopotamia in Room 227. It's as mythic as the Rosetta Stone. We recommend strolling through the sculpture courtyard.
